ACTIVITY DESCRIPTION: The P4: Pacific Regional Connectivity Program: Phase 3B, FJ Connectivity Project (P159297) is a World Bank-supported initiative, identified as a Project Appraisal Document (Report No: PAD1968) issued on November 7, 2016, by the International Bank for Reconstruction and Development (IBRD). This regionally tagged project, part of a series, involves Fiji, Micronesia (Federated States of), Kiribati, Palau, Tonga, Tuvalu, and Samoa, with the Republic of Fiji as the borrower and the Ministry of Communications as the implementing agency. It is financed by a proposed IBRD loan of US$5.95 million, contributing to a total project cost of US$6.36 million, with the remaining US$0.41 million provided by the Borrower, indicating no financing gap. The fiscal year for the project runs from August 1 to July 31, and currency equivalents as of October 28, 2016, set FJD at US$0.49 and US$1 at FJD 2.04. The project was approved on November 30, 2016, with a closing date of December 16, 2021. Its proposed development objectives are to reduce the cost and increase the availability of internet services in the Northern Division of Fiji's territory.

The project comprises three components: a Submarine Cable System, budgeted at US$5.20 million; Regulatory Technical Assistance, costing US$0.20 million; and Project Management and Administration, allocated US$0.55 million. Expected annual disbursements are US$2.50 million in 2017, US$2.75 million in 2018, and US$0.70 million in 2019, with no further disbursements planned for 2020-2022. The lead practice area for the project is Transport & ICT, with no Bank/IFC collaboration. A gender analysis to identify relevant gaps between males and females is planned, though specific actions to address them or indicators in the results framework are not included. The project's Environmental Assessment Category is B - Partial Assessment.

For forecasting overall activity success, the Systematic Operations Risk-Rating Tool (SORT) indicates an overall Moderate risk, with specific ratings as follows: Political and Governance (Moderate), Macroeconomic (Moderate), Sector Strategies and Policies (Low), Technical Design of Project or Program (Moderate), Institutional Capacity for Implementation and Sustainability (Moderate), Fiduciary (Moderate), Environment and Social (Low), Stakeholders (Moderate), and Other (Substantial). The project does not depart from the Country Partnership Framework (CPF) nor does it require any waivers of Bank policies. Several safeguard policies are triggered, including Environmental Assessment (OP/BP 4.01), Natural Habitats (OP/BP 4.04), Physical Cultural Resources (OP/BP 4.11), Indigenous Peoples (OP/BP 4.10), Involuntary Resettlement (OP/BP 4.12), Safety of Dams (OP/BP 4.37), and Projects on International Waterways (OP/BP 7.50).

Legal covenants stipulate that the Borrower shall establish and maintain a Project Management Unit (PMU) in the Ministry of Communications within three months of the Effective Date, ensuring it has adequate staff and consultants with satisfactory qualifications and experience. The PMU is responsible for technical and administrative project coordination, supervision, planning, reporting, monitoring and evaluation, communication, and outreach, requiring competencies in technical project management, project coordination, procurement, financial management/accounting, and safeguard support. The Borrower is also required to procure the submarine cable and ancillary equipment for the "Savusavu Spur" and an Indefeasible Right of Use (IRU) for at least 25 years on the SSCC main submarine cable, with both agreements designed to ensure cross-dependency, open access on transparent and non-discriminatory terms, assignability of rights, and compliance with Anti-Corruption Guidelines. ACTIVITY ID: 44000-P159515

ACTIVITY DESCRIPTION: The "Argentina Access to Longer Term Finance for Micro, Small and Medium Enterprises Project" (P159515), an Investment Project Financing project by The World Bank, was approved on November 17, 2016, with a closing date of November 30, 2021. It is not regionally tagged, but focuses on Argentina, and involves Financial Intermediaries with an Environmental Assessment Category of 'F - Financial Intermediary Assessment'. The project involves Bank/IFC Collaboration at a Joint Level, indicating a complementary or interdependent project requiring active coordination. The Project Development Objectives (PDOs) are to improve access to, and strengthen the framework for the provision of, longer-term finance for eligible micro, small, and medium enterprises (MSMEs).

The project is structured around three components. The first is a Line of credit, costing USD 44.50 million, which will provide USD 44.5 million equivalent to BICE (Banco de Inversión y Comercio Exterior) for on-lending through Participating Financial Institutions (PFIs) to MSMEs. The second component, valued at USD 4.55 million, focuses on institutional capacity building of BICE, financial infrastructure reforms, establishing a credit guarantee fund, and capacity building for MSMEs. This aims to enhance BICE's second-tier operations, provide technical assistance for improving the enabling environment for finance access, and build capacity for MSMEs themselves. The third component, Project management, allocates USD 0.82 million to support the Project Implementation Unit's capacity to implement the project.

The rationale for the project stems from significant challenges faced by MSMEs in Argentina. MSMEs contribute substantially to the economy, accounting for at least 50 percent of GDP, about 71 percent of total employment, and 47 percent of total sales, though data on these figures may be higher today. Despite this, only 38 percent of formal small firms had a loan or line of credit in 2010, significantly lower than medium and large firms. MSME informality (over 40 percent of GDP) further limits the banking sector's willingness to lend due to a lack of reliable financial statements. Access to bank credit for industrial MSMEs is limited to about a third, dominated by short-term working capital loans, with about 90 percent of credit to MSMEs being short term. The majority of MSMEs rely on internal funds, hindering their capacity to invest and grow. In Argentina, 64 percent of loans have maturities below one year, and a third of industrial MSMEs reportedly stopped investment projects due to credit unavailability. The lack of financing over a year prevents MSMEs from replacing obsolete equipment, leading to a loss of production capacity, while long-term finance is generally associated with improved company performance, faster growth, productivity, and profitability.

The project is timely as Argentina's new Government is spearheading structural reforms in the financial sector, including the elimination of caps on consumer and credit card lending rates, removal of floors on deposit rates, and easing of conditions for mandated lending under the Line of Credit for Productive Investments (LCIP) program. The Government has also highlighted the importance of financial infrastructure reforms and the development of non-traditional financial products for MSMEs, such as reforms in secured transactions, insolvency and creditor rights, and the development of factoring, leasing, Mutual Guarantee Associations (SGRs), and the revamp of the public credit guarantee scheme (FOGAPYME). This proposed pilot project is designed to assist authorities in laying the groundwork for significant financial sector reforms, being the first World Bank operation in the sector since the 2001/2002 financial crisis. The operation aims to provide the banking sector with long-term local currency finance, currently scarce, which will allow for extended loan maturities for MSMEs, implement a new collaborative model with BICE and the banking sector, address structural financial sector deficiencies like weak financial infrastructure, and evaluate the banking sector's interest in serving new MSME segments. The World Bank brings a combination of long-term financing, expertise in financial intermediary lending operations, and best practices in financial infrastructure reforms and environmental and social management. MSMEs are the final beneficiaries, expecting to gain improved access to longer-term finance and benefits from enhanced financial infrastructure that will facilitate credit access, potentially leading to increased productivity through long-term fixed investments. The project will monitor MSME performance indicators such as employment, sales, and exports.

For forecasting overall activity success, the Project Development Objective Indicators include reaching a ratio of 1.00 for the average portfolio maturity of MSME sub-loans under the project compared to PFIs' MSME lending portfolio not financed by the project, establishing a dedicated unit at BICE for risk management of second-tier operations with improved capacity (End Target: 1.00, up from 0.00 baseline for both), and developing and functioning a new business development service platform operated by SEPYME (End Target: 1.00, up from 0.00 baseline). Intermediate Results Indicators target cumulative Bank Support for Lines of Credit - SME at USD 53,000,000.00 by year five (up from 0.00 baseline) and a cumulative number of 106 sub-loans provided by selected PFIs to MSMEs with Project funds (up from 0.00 baseline). These indicators suggest a focus on increasing the maturity of financing, strengthening institutional capacity for financial intermediation, improving support platforms for businesses, and scaling up the volume and number of sub-loans to MSMEs.

ACTIVITY DESCRIPTION: "The PforR, titled \"Economic Opportunities for Jordanians and Syrian Refugees,\" aims to improve economic opportunities for both Jordanians and Syrian refugees. This is to be achieved by reforming Jordan's labor market regulations to grant Syrian refugees access to the formal labor market, allowing them to legally contribute to the economy. The program also focuses on improving Jordan's investment climate by enhancing regulatory predictability, reducing red tape, supporting small businesses, and facilitating trade. A significant component involves attracting and retaining both domestic and foreign investments, especially in manufacturing within Special Economic Zones (SEZs) that offer preferential access to the EU market. Anticipated foreign investors include the Syrian business diaspora, regional investors, and those targeting the EU market. Economic modeling suggests a positive cost-benefit ratio for this program, stemming from an improved business environment, lower compliance costs for firms, increased firm productivity, and ultimately, greater investments and job creation. The program's scope, aligned with the Jordan Compact, covers investment climate reform, investment promotion, and labor market reform, while excluding other Compact topics like Jordan Response Plan initiatives, education for Syrian refugee children, vocational training, and macroeconomic stability measures. Key activities include issuing work permits for Syrians by changing labor market regulations, publishing labor compliance dashboards in manufacturing, establishing a predictability process for laws and regulations, simplifying regulations for household businesses and SMEs, expanding the Customs Golden List, removing minimum capital requirements for foreign investments, and modernizing investment promotion and aftercare services. These efforts are expected to lead to greater mobility and formal labor market participation for Syrians, improved labor conditions in SEZs, enhanced regulatory predictability and transparency, reduced red tape, increased household businesses, and improved Customs operations, all contributing to increased and retained investments in Jordan and the creation of formal, decent jobs. Despite efforts, between 90,000 and 130,000 Syrian refugees continue to work illegally due to challenges such as the nature of available jobs (often seasonal or daily in agriculture and construction), the requirement for one-year contracts, and employers' reluctance to navigate bureaucratic processes and formal registration. The program's Disbursement-Linked Indicator (DLI) for labor focuses on the number of annual work permits granted, which may be achieved through policy actions faster than through job creation alone. Investment climate reforms are crucial, addressing ongoing concerns about regulatory predictability, stability, interpretation, and the burdensome, costly regime of numerous licenses and inspections, with trade reforms also vital for maximizing benefits from the EU trade deal. To enhance predictability, a prime minister's decree on consultation guidelines for new or changing regulations is planned, alongside simplifying licensing for home-based businesses and expanding the Customs Golden List/Trusted Trader Program. Investment promotion is critical for job generation, with strategies centered on SEZs, the Syrian business diaspora, regional investors, and EU market-focused investors. Budget data indicates a planned 2% increase for 2019-2020 to match inflation, contrasting with a decrease in budgets from 2016-2018."

ACTIVITY ID: 44000-P159669

ACTIVITY DESCRIPTION: The "Second Programmatic Electricity Distribution Reform DPL for Rajasthan" activity, with Operation ID P159669 and an overall risk rating of Substantial, is a Development Policy Financing (DPF) operation for India, specifically targeting the state of Rajasthan. The Republic of India is the Borrower, with the Energy Department, Government of Rajasthan (GoR) as the Implementing Agency. This is the second in a series of two single-tranche programmatic development policy operations, with an IBRD Loan amount of US$250 million. The proposed operation, which has an estimated Board Date of March 29, 2018, aims to continue supporting the GoR's program for the turnaround of the distribution sector in Rajasthan under the 24x7 Power for All program. Its Program Development Objectives are to support the turnaround of the electricity distribution sector in Rajasthan by: (a) strengthening the governance framework, (b) enhancing policies to restructure its finances, and (c) improving its operational performance. This operation builds upon foundations laid by the first operation, carrying forward financial restructuring and deepening reforms for operational performance improvement. The reform strategy is centered on two key frameworks: UDAY (Ujwal DISCOM Assurance Yojana), which Rajasthan joined in January 2016, and the Rajasthan State Electricity Distribution Management Responsibility (RSEDMR) Act, enacted in January 2016 and approved on March 31, 2016, making Rajasthan the first Indian state to employ such an instrument.

Key results indicators for forecasting overall activity success include: the disbursement of an incentive for performance in FY19 (target by September 2019); the date of availability of audited annual accounts by September 30, 2019 (zero-month delay from baseline December 31, 2015 with a three-month delay); annual loss of DISCOMs to be taken over and funded by the State under UDAY Program, targeting 10% in FY19 from a baseline of 0% in FY15; a reduction in the gap between Average Cost of Supply (ACoS) and Average Revenue Realised (ARR) to INR 0.70/kWh in FY19 from INR 3.00/kWh in FY15; a reduction in Aggregate Technical and Commercial (AT&C) losses to 17% (a 12.5 percentage point reduction) by FY19 from 29.5% in FY15; an increase in the number of consumers on pre-paid/Automatic Meter Reading (AMR)/Advanced Metering Infrastructure (AMI) meters to 100,000 by September 2019 from 0 in FY15; the distribution of 16,000,000 LED lamps by September 2019 from 0 in FY15; an increase in the percentage of positions filled in IT cadre in DISCOMs to 75% by September 2019 (with a gender disaggregated target of 15% female share) from 0% in FY15; and a reduction in the number of unelectrified households in the State to 750,000 by September 2019 from 2,182,180 as of October 10, 2017. Steps already implemented toward UDAY and RSEDMR Act include the GoR taking over 75 percent of DISCOMs' debt, signing performance Memoranda of Understanding (MoUs) between the state government and DISCOMs for FY17/18, and inducting independent directors to the Board of Directors of DISCOMs and laying a code of conduct for members. The proposed programmatic series is designed to support the GoR's Vision 2020 development program, centered around three main pillars: encouraging investment and accelerating economic development, generating employment and maintaining good governance, and promoting social justice and citizen empowerment.

ACTIVITY ID: 44000-P159712

ACTIVITY DESCRIPTION: The Sindh Solar Energy Project (P159712) in Pakistan is an Investment Project Financing initiative with an Environmental Assessment Category of B-Partial Assessment. It was expected to be approved on June 14, 2018, with an anticipated closing date of September 29, 2023. The project involves joint-level collaboration with the Bank/IFC, indicating a complementary or interdependent project requiring active coordination. The project's Proposed Development Objective is to increase solar power generation and access to electricity in Sindh Province.

The project addresses critical issues within Pakistan's power sector, which suffers from a lack of commercial discipline and operational effectiveness, evidenced by high technical and nontechnical losses (around 18%) and low collections (around 94%). This has contributed to a chronic liquidity crisis, with accumulated circular debt reaching an estimated PKR374 billion (US$3.4 billion) by December 2016. Inadequate power supply and poor-quality electricity service have reduced GDP growth by approximately two percentage points annually. Historically, investment in power infrastructure declined, and new private sector investment halted from the mid-1990s to FY09/10. Despite an installed capacity of about 31,000 MW, generation capacity shortages persist, with an actual shortfall of 5,000–7,000 MW against a peak demand of 29,000 MW in June 2017. This results in significant load shedding for both households and industrial consumers. The country's electricity mix is increasingly reliant on imported fossil fuels, making it vulnerable to price volatility and contributing 46 percent of total greenhouse gas emissions. Despite a conducive renewable energy policy since 2006 and significant solar and wind resource potential in the south and west, installed solar and wind power capacity remained relatively low at 430 MW and 940 MW, respectively, at the time of project appraisal. Regulatory changes concerning "up-front tariffs" for solar and wind were noted in 2017 after their provision until 2016.

The project’s strong development rationale aligns with Pakistan's Nationally Determined Contribution to reduce greenhouse gas emissions, its 2025 Vision, and the 2006 Policy for Development of Renewable Energy for Power Generation. It aims to reduce load shedding and the cost of electricity production through new solar power capacity, which is now a highly competitive option. The project incorporates measures on gender, ensures 100 percent climate co-benefits, and employs a Maximizing Finance for Development approach by emphasizing private sector delivery and leveraging commercial finance. The World Bank brings experience from similar solar power development and electricity access programs in Argentina, Armenia, India, Zambia, Bangladesh, and Kenya.

As per a Memorandum of Understanding dated December 10, 2021, involving the Energy Department, Government of Sindh, K-Electric Limited, and the World Bank, the project includes specific components and results targets, following the execution of financing and project agreements on January 9, 2019. Component 1 aims for 400 MW of Utility-Scale Solar to be developed by the private sector under a competitive bidding model, while Component 2 targets 20 MW of Distributed Solar on and around public buildings. The final project beneficiaries are Pakistani consumers and households, who will gain from low-cost, secure, and clean electricity. Direct beneficiaries include the Sindh Energy Department (SED), which will attract private sector investment, develop competitive bidding regimes and solar parks, implement distributed solar PV schemes, leverage private sector provision of Solar Home Systems (SHSs) to increase electricity access, and improve its monitoring and evaluation capacity. Private sector Independent Power Producers (IPPs) will gain opportunities for investment in de-risked solar power projects. Public sector agencies and Distribution Companies (DISCOs) in Sindh will benefit from reduced public sector electricity bills through distributed solar PV installations, thereby helping to reduce the circular debt. Finally, households in Sindh with no or low levels of electricity access will benefit from the provision of SHSs. The project's success will be influenced by its ability to effectively implement competitive bidding, attract private sector investment in a challenging financial environment, and achieve its ambitious solar power generation and access targets in Sindh Province.

ACTIVITY ID: 44000-P159807

ACTIVITY DESCRIPTION: "The Power System Reliability and Efficiency Improvement Project (P159807) aims to improve the reliability and efficiency of the power system in Bangladesh through optimization of dispatch operation. This objective supports the progressive implementation of primary/secondary frequency controls, investments in participating generators, transmission bottleneck removals, and the institutionalization of merit order dispatch. The project aligns with the World Bank's goals of ending extreme poverty and boosting shared prosperity, and its Energy Sector Direction Paper, which emphasizes affordable, reliable, and sustainable energy supply. It also addresses electricity as the second highest constraint for Bangladeshi businesses, as highlighted by the 2015 Systematic Country Diagnostic. Furthermore, the project is consistent with the Country Partnership Framework for FY 2016-2020, which prioritizes increasing electricity and gas supply, diversifying power sources, retiring polluting diesel generators, and reducing energy subsidies, while also aligning with the Government's power sector strategy to ameliorate power shortages and increase the efficient use of scarce domestic gas. The project, financed by the IDA Scale Up Facility, is deemed economically viable, expected to generate significant savings in operating expenditures, primarily fuel, and additional revenues from reduced unserved energy. Project interventions involve retrofitting to an automated, modernized dispatch function, coupled with transmission upgrading, operational enhancements, and technical assistance (TA). These interventions are designed to improve system security and reliability by minimizing outages and preventing wider blackouts, and to increase the efficiency and cost-effectiveness of system operation by ensuring adherence to merit-order dispatch. Expected beneficiaries include the Government of Bangladesh through fiscal savings from reduced fuel use, all grid-connected consumers who will experience fewer interruptions and voltage fluctuations, households with less appliance destruction, businesses and industrial enterprises requiring fewer back-up generators, and power distribution entities with improved performance due to reduced frequency fluctuations. Generators will also benefit from more reliable and stable system operation, with fewer start-ups and network spikes. Additionally, the reduction in oil-fired generation will decrease greenhouse gas emissions and improve public health through reduced air pollution. The project's success will be measured by key indicators, including a reduction in unserved energy due to underfrequency from a baseline of 5.08 Gigawatt-hour (GWh) to an end target of 2.08 GWh, and achieving a projected fuel saving of 1500506.7 Mega Joules (MJ) from a baseline of 0.00 MJ. Intermediate outcome indicators include minimum/maximum frequency, number of staff trained, number of generation units with upgraded governors and excitation systems, number of Dynamic Line Ratings (DLRs) installed, kilometers of transmission lines constructed or rehabilitated, cumulative duration and number of power outages per year due to under-frequency, percentage of female staff trained, and citizen engagement events on power supply quality."

ACTIVITY ID: 44000-P159844

ACTIVITY DESCRIPTION: "The Togo Fiscal Reform Credit aims for a significant fiscal adjustment, targeting a primary fiscal surplus of 2 percent of GDP (0.5 percent after arrears payment) and an overall fiscal deficit below 1 percent of GDP (2.2 percent after arrears payment) by 2019, which is below the WAEMU convergence criterion. This entails a fiscal adjustment, as measured by the primary balance excluding bank recapitalization, of 9 percentage points of GDP from 2016 to 2019 (7.7 percentage points after arrears payment). The 2018 budget is being prepared with further significant fiscal consolidation, projecting a reduction in the primary deficit (excluding bank recapitalization) from 2.7 percent of GDP in 2017 to 2.2 percent of GDP in 2018, including 2.0 percent for arrears payment. An exceptional but necessary fiscal cost for restructuring two public banks is expected to increase the projected deficit in 2018, which will be financed by additional borrowing on the regional market. \n\nThe Government is actively pursuing efforts to improve revenue collection and contain spending. Total tax revenue is anticipated to increase from 16.9 percent of GDP in 2016 to 19.8 percent in 2019, primarily due to efficiency gains from the operationalization of the Revenue Authority (OTR), broadening the tax base, and rationalizing tax expenditures. Tax reforms are also designed to attract new economic operators to the Lomé sea port. Nontax revenue is projected to remain at its current level of 1.9 percent of GDP. Spending is programmed to decline from 31.4 percent of GDP in 2016 to 25.7 percent of GDP in 2019, mainly through continued reductions in domestically financed public investment, which will decrease from 14 percent of GDP in 2016 to 7.9 percent in 2019. The wage bill is set to be rationalized by 0.1 percent of GDP annually to reach 6.7 percent of GDP by 2019, and fuel subsidies will be contained by improving efficiency and lowering generation costs in the energy sector. To mitigate the impact of cuts in public investment on the poor, the Government plans to slightly increase pro-poor social spending. \n\nThe latest Debt Sustainability Analysis (2017) indicates that Togo faces a moderate risk of external debt distress but is at a heightened risk of overall debt distress, reflecting its high level of total public debt. While external debt sustainability ratios are expected to remain below indicative thresholds throughout the projected period (2017-2035) under a baseline scenario, historical scenarios and stress tests suggest thresholds would be breached in later years, indicating Togo's vulnerability to adverse shocks. A targeted primary fiscal surplus, combined with growth in the 5 percent range, is expected to put public debt, including public enterprise debt, on a firm downward trajectory, reaching 73.5 percent of GDP by end-2019 (69.5 percent excluding bank recapitalization). Assuming a 2 percent of GDP primary surplus is sustained in the post-IMF program period, the present value of public debt could be brought below the 38 percent of GDP threshold for heightened risk of public sector debt by 2025. The overall debt dynamics highlight the vulnerability of the Togolese economy to certain shocks, such as low exports, declining commodity prices, less favorable financing terms, and lower revenue. This underscores the need for a prudent approach to new borrowing, including the continuation of a zero-limit on non-concessional borrowing, and the importance of improving debt management capacity for realistic assessments of risks, particularly in the infrastructure sector. Significant tensions in the DSA may arise if the government is required to provide guarantees for new private sector investment, for example, in the energy sector. \n\nThe fiscal management reforms encompass specific prior actions and triggers. For revenue mobilization, prior actions for FMIR 1 included completing an audit of tax exemptions to identify and eliminate irregularities, initiating measures in the 2018 Budget Act to reduce regular tax exemptions (including on VAT), launching a new system for electronic tax filing by companies under the Large Taxpayers Department, and implementing a program for micro and small enterprises to enable tax payments through mobile phones. Triggers for FMIR 2 involve amending the Tax Code to rationalize taxes in line with WAEMU fiscal directives (on corporate tax, VAT, and excise tax) without revenue loss, increasing property tax revenues by reducing inefficiencies in land administration (including computerizing the land registry), and harmonizing Customs and Domestic Taxes databases to improve taxpayer identification and monitoring. For strengthening public investment management, prior actions for FMIR 1 included establishing a high-level inter-ministerial Public Investment Committee to scrutinize and prioritize all public investment proposals, including Public-Private Partnerships (PPPs), using rigorous standardized economic appraisal criteria, which incorporate social and environmental impacts. Additionally, outstanding obligations to commercial banks for past pre-financed investments were settled through the issuance of securities with longer maturities and lower interest rates. A trigger for FMIR 2 is to include a three-year rolling Public Investment Plan in the 2019 Budget Act, with all priority projects (including PPPs identified by the Public Investment Committee in coordination with the PPP unit) selected based on proper feasibility, cost-benefit, and value-for-money analysis. \n\nReforms in the electricity sector are a critical component, aiming to improve the financial situation of CEET to expand its operations and ensure greater access for poor households, given that only 13.9 percent of the 45.5 percent poor population are CEET's customers. The current social policy of subsidizing tariffs per kWh for small consumers is deemed ineffective. A new tariff policy based solely on usage in kWh, without fixed kVa fees for small customers, is expected to improve the welfare of the poor, boost electricity consumption, liberate household resources, and contribute to reducing the electricity poverty rate (currently 37.4 percent) and poverty in general, potentially helping 2.4 percent of connected poor households escape poverty. Eliminating fixed costs for small consumers (up to 40 kWh) would reduce the price of the first 40 prepaid kWh, increasing poor and vulnerable households' readiness to connect to the grid, which would favor productivity, income growth, women's labor market participation, and reduce the use of environmentally and health-detrimental energy sources. Prepaid meters are encouraged for expenditure control, and geographical targeting (e.g., based on INSEED poverty rates by communes) is proposed to further enhance benefits for poor households. Prior actions include ensuring ministries and general government agencies reduce the risk of not allocating sufficient budget for the Ministry of Finance to pay electricity bills and improving the financial situation of CEET by reducing the gap between service unit cost and revenues per kWh. Triggers for FMIR 2 involve developing a transparent methodology and institutional framework for determining CEB's cost-reflective wholesale power price, which has been frozen since 2013 and does not account for transmission costs; improving CEET's tariff-setting procedures to ensure the utility can cover its costs, including transparent procedures for estimating necessary fiscal subsidies if tariffs cannot be increased; adopting and implementing a revenue protection program for medium and high-voltage customers; and adopting a methodology for the development of a least-cost power system expansion plan to provide clear prioritization of investments. Achieving financial viability in the electricity sector is viewed as a medium-term undertaking that requires careful planning and monitoring. \n\nAdditionally, fostering competition and increased private sector participation in the ICT sector, along with an improved regulatory environment, are expected to improve service delivery for households and private businesses. This should enhance the productivity of Togolese businesses, stimulating growth and increasing the demand for labor by addressing inefficiencies that have historically prevented firms from utilizing expanded mobile telephony and internet services. \n\nThe proposed FMIR program is anticipated to have significant positive impacts. Revenue mobilization actions (Prior Actions 1 & 2, Triggers 1 & 2) are expected to have a long-term indirect positive impact on poverty through increased fiscal space and expanded government services, encouraging private sector investment and job creation, particularly among small and medium-sized enterprises, by simplifying the tax system and facilitating payments. The rationalization of tax exemptions is focused on eliminating irregularities and improper extensions, with minimal direct impact on the poor as food imports are not targeted, and tax simplification reforms aim to be progressive. Strengthening public investment planning and implementation (Prior Actions 3 & 4, Trigger 3) is expected to have long-term indirect positive impacts on poverty reduction by increasing allocative effectiveness and efficiency of public investment resources, leading to improved infrastructure services for the population, including the poorest households. Improvements in the electricity sector (Prior Actions 5 & 6, Triggers 4, 5 & 6) are expected to have substantial long-term positive direct and indirect impacts on poor households by restoring financial viability without increasing tariffs for lower-income households, attracting needed investment, improving access and quality of services, liberating family time, boosting productivity, and fostering firm creation and employment, with reduced government transfers freeing up budgetary resources for pro-poor activities. A Poverty and Social Impact Assessment on electricity tariff reforms, though not yet publicly released, provided recommendations to be pursued by CEET and authorities. Most prior actions and triggers are considered environmentally neutral, with minor concerns in the electricity and ICT sectors being secondary, as they improve the investment environment rather than directly supporting new investments. These combined efforts aim to improve Togo's fiscal health, reduce debt, and foster inclusive growth."

ACTIVITY ID: 44000-P159870

ACTIVITY DESCRIPTION: The Zhejiang Qiandao Lake and Xin'an River Basin Water Resources and Ecological Environment Protection Project (P159870) is an Investment Project Financing initiative approved on June 6, 2018, with a closing date of June 30, 2024. This project is not regionally tagged and does not involve Bank/IFC collaboration. It is classified under Environmental Assessment Category B - Partial Assessment. The proposed development objective is to strengthen integrated pollution and watershed management and increase access to improved water supply in selected landscapes, supporting Zhejiang's program for the protection of Qiandao Lake and Xin'an River Basin. The People's Republic of China is the borrower, with the Zhejiang Provincial Construction Department serving as the implementing agency.

The project addresses critical environmental challenges, specifically the high non-point source pollution from agriculture, which contributes 60-70 percent of ammonia nitrogen, phosphorus, and nitrate discharges due to unregulated manure disposal and improper use of fertilizers and herbicides. This pollution has degraded water quality in Qiandao Lake, evidenced by an increase in total nitrogen from 0.82mg/l to 1.23mg/l between 2010 and 2015, a rise in trophic state indices, and reduced water transparency, raising concerns given the lake's economic importance and its role as a primary source of drinking water. To counter this, the project aims to reduce nonpoint pollution cost-effectively, restore degraded ecosystems, develop long-term management mechanisms for pollution and natural resources, and enhance coordination among stakeholders, as requested by the Government of Zhejiang (GoZ) for sustainable interventions.

The total project cost is estimated at US$293.46 million, comprising an IBRD loan of US$150.00 million (approximately 51% of the total project cost) and US$143.46 million in local counterpart funding. The IBRD loan is a US Dollar denominated, commitment-linked variable spread loan with a repayment period of 25 years, including a 6-year grace period. The project's baseline costs amount to US$263.65 million, with US$142.26 million financed by IBRD. It is structured into three main components: Component 1: Landscape Management Improvement, with a total cost of US$101.54 million (US$81.79 million IBRD financed); Component 2: Water Resources Management Improvement, totaling US$152.41 million (US$51.68 million IBRD financed); and Component 3: Institutional Capacity Building, Monitoring and Project Management, costing US$9.70 million (US$8.79 million IBRD financed).

Component 1, Landscape Management Improvement, focuses heavily on Climate Smart Agriculture (CSA) practices to address nonpoint pollution from agricultural crop production. Activities include developing and disseminating soil nutrient management plans for optimized fertilizer application and efficiency, upgrading Integrated Pest Management (IPM) systems, and demonstrating low-impact soil tillage and minimum tillage practices for conservation agriculture to mitigate soil erosion, runoff, nutrient loss, and carbon releases. Specific measures under this component also entail the use of vegetation barriers, ecological interception ditches, collection and recycling of 90% of used agrochemical containers, banning toxic herbicides and insecticides, and promoting organic agriculture through the application of specified tonnages of slow-release and organic fertilizers. Sub-components include Low Impact Crop Production (US$31.81 million), Livestock Waste Management (US$1.00 million), Plantation Restoration and Rehabilitation, and Forestry Infrastructure (US$68.29 million), and Wetland Management (US$0.44 million).

The project is closely aligned with the FY 2013-2016 World Bank Group's Country Partnership Strategy for China, emphasizing sustainable natural resources management. It supports China's national ecological conservation and water management strategies, as well as the GoZ's Integrated Plan for Water Resources and Eco-Environment Protection and the 13th Five-Year Plan for Zhejiang Province. This initiative is part of a larger government watershed management program, including the Yangtze River Economic Belt Eco-civilization Program, with the GoZ planning an investment of approximately US$2.1 billion. The project aims to promote climate-smart agriculture, environmentally sustainable forest management, integrated landscape management, multisector cooperation, ecosystem restoration, and cross-border collaboration between Anhui and Zhejiang Provinces. It will also pioneer innovative watershed assessment and management models and a payments for ecological services (PES) mechanism, generating valuable lessons for other large-scale investments in China.

Key beneficiaries include approximately 720,000 farmers, forest plantation owners, livestock producers, companies engaged in low-impact agriculture and tree planting, and residents receiving improved wastewater management and potable water supply in Chun'an County and Jiande City. Indirect beneficiaries include residents around Qiandao Lake and tourists, and the project will generate employment through various construction activities. Project success will be measured by PDO-level results indicators: nitrogen load reduction, phosphorus load reduction, decrease in soil erosion, and the number of people gaining access to improved water sources. The project's design incorporates an ecosystem-based adaptation approach to strengthen landscape resilience against climate change impacts such as intense rainfall, drought, erosion, and sediment.

For forecasting overall activity success, several elements are highly favorable. The strong alignment with national and provincial development plans, coupled with significant government financial commitment to a broader watershed management program (US$2.1 billion), indicates robust institutional backing. The project's emphasis on innovative, scalable approaches like Climate Smart Agriculture, Integrated Pest Management, and Payments for Ecological Services, along with explicit knowledge sharing objectives, suggests a forward-thinking and potentially highly effective intervention model. The proactive integration of climate change considerations and ecosystem-based adaptation strategies enhances its resilience and long-term impact. Furthermore, clear identification of beneficiaries, measurable results indicators, and dedicated components for institutional capacity building, monitoring, and evaluation provide a strong framework for accountability and adaptive management. The substantial co-financing from the World Bank and local governments underscores a strong financial commitment.

ACTIVITY ID: 44000-P159956

ACTIVITY DESCRIPTION: The "Green Resilient Growth DPF," formally known as the First Programmatic Green Growth Development Policy Operation, is a series of three single-tranche programmatic Development Policy Financing operations. The borrower is the Lao People's Democratic Republic (Lao PDR), with the Ministry of Planning and Investment serving as the implementation agency. It is financed by an IDA Credit of SDR 28.5 million (equivalent to US$38.6 million), with IDA blend terms including a 25-year maturity, 5 years grace, a 1.25% interest charge per year, and a 0.75% service charge per year. The overarching Program Development Objective is to support the Government of Lao PDR in achieving fiscal stability and consolidating its pathway towards green growth.

The activity's success will be measured through various result indicators across three pillars. Pillar 1, focusing on strengthening prospects for fiscal sustainability and financial sector stability, aims to increase fiscal consolidation supported by higher tax revenues from a baseline of 14.6% of GDP (FY15/16) to a target of 16.7% of GDP (FY20), and lower the wage bill from 8.4% to 7.4% of GDP over the same period. It also targets a reduction in public debt from 68% of GDP (2016) to 65% of GDP (2020), and public reporting of compliance with regulations on the Capital Adequacy Ratio by type of bank, targeting at least the regulatory minimum from a baseline of not reported. Pillar 2, consolidating green growth principles across the national development strategy, seeks the disclosure of monitoring and evaluation results of green growth priorities in the 9th National Socio-Economic Development Plan (2021-2025) and an increase in revenue sources for the Environment Protection Fund (EPF) from 3 to 5. It also targets the disclosure of the annual financial audit of the EPF, the undertaking of one strategic environmental assessment (SEA) for public policies, programs, and strategic plans, and increasing the share of investment projects in mining, energy, and transport sectors requiring an Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA) with full disclosure to 70% of Group 2 projects. Additionally, 50% of approved road maintenance projects are targeted to include climate resilience and disaster risks considerations, verified by technical audits. Pillar 3, incorporating green growth in selected sectors, aims for one river basin to meet minimum flow requirements outlined in its respective river basin plan, from a baseline of zero.

This DPF series is designed to support a package of reforms to strengthen the GoL's capacity for clean, resource-efficient, and resilient growth by integrating green growth principles into the national development strategy and specific sectors. Pillar 1 addresses macroeconomic risks, Pillar 2 provides a cross-sectoral foundation for improved planning, and Pillar 3 initiates targeted actions to reduce environmental impacts in hydropower, logging, and agriculture, while ensuring sustainable growth. The program is also expected to strengthen the green growth dialogue and accountability mechanisms, promoting the country's branding as "Green, Clean, and Beautiful Lao PDR," which can foster new growth sectors like tourism and green agricultural and forestry products. Advisory services will complement the DPF to mitigate risks related to institutional capacity and program complexity by providing technical expertise, developing policy frameworks, and facilitating interagency coordination. The broader World Bank-financed portfolio will also provide support.

Forecasting success is influenced by several factors. The economic outlook for the medium term is broadly positive, contingent on improved macroeconomic management, strengthened power sector planning, and business environment reforms. Specific fiscal reforms, such as revising import duties, removing fuel tax exemptions, and amending the VAT law, have already been undertaken, with efforts supported by the DPF expected to strengthen the fiscal position and reduce public debt. However, significant challenges remain, including a high external deficit, low foreign exchange reserves (around 3 months of imports), and public and private external debt nearing 98% of GDP in 2016, exposing the country to external shocks. A recognized weakness in current planning and decision-making for public and private investments is the lack of rigorous integration of green growth considerations, which the DPF aims to address by introducing a legal requirement for SEAs. Prior actions taken by the GoL, such as mandating MONRE to prepare sectoral SEAs and require SEAs in the formulation of policies and plans, demonstrate commitment. The introduction of an integrated river-basin management (IWRM) approach, along with the development of river-basin management plans and guidelines for water resource assessment and planning, is crucial given the current lack of an organized regime, growing demand, and transboundary water user competition, which the DPF series also seeks to strengthen. The complexity of establishing minimum flow requirements and the fact that the target SEA is the first GoL-led non-traditional SEA indicate ambitious undertakings, requiring sustained institutional capacity building and quality control.

ACTIVITY ID: 44000-P159969

ACTIVITY DESCRIPTION: The Second Public Investment Reform Support Credit (PIRSC2), identified by Operation ID P159969 and Report No. 112711, is a Development Policy Lending operation for Niger, located in the AFRICA region. The project addresses central government administration (50%), general energy (25%), and irrigation and drainage (25%). The Republic of Niger is the borrower, with the Ministry of Finance serving as the implementing agency. The Program Information Document (PID) was prepared on October 12, 2016, with an estimated appraisal date of January 24, 2017, and an estimated Board approval date of February 28, 2017. Following a corporate review, the decision was made to proceed with the operation's preparation.

The PIRSC's development objectives include improving the quality, reliability, and accountability of the country's Public Finance Management (PFM) and Public Investment Management (PIM) systems, establishing the necessary policy and regulatory framework for a well-functioning public irrigation sector, and facilitating the reform of the electricity sector. This operation proposes a US$50 million equivalent credit to the Government of Niger. It is the second and final credit in the PIRSC series, which was originally envisioned as three single-tranche budget support credits to be disbursed over 2016-2018, with IDA17 programming US$150 million for the combined operations. The series is being closed earlier than planned at the Government's request due to a changing country context and the emergence of new challenges, which will be addressed by the upcoming Economic Development Document (EDD). The proposed operation is structured around three pillars: strengthening PFM and PIM systems, improving public irrigation sector management, and enhancing the institutional framework for the electricity sector. The PIRSC series supports Pillars 1, 3, and 4 of Niger's current national poverty reduction strategy, the Plan de Développement Economique et Social (PDES), which focuses on strengthening public institutions, reinforcing food security and agricultural development, and promoting diversification and competitiveness.

The operation takes place amid a deteriorating global and regional environment that has negatively impacted Niger's recent economic performance. While Niger experienced high growth (7 percent in 2014) between 2010 and 2014, driven by agriculture and natural resource projects, economic activity slowed to 3.5 percent in 2015 due to reduced agricultural growth, lower oil and mining outputs, security developments, and spillovers from Nigeria. The fiscal deficit also increased significantly due to rising security spending, lower natural resource revenues, and a rapid increase in public investment, highlighting the need for improved public investment management efficiency. Despite these challenges, Niger continues to strengthen its democratic governance, with renewed executive and parliamentarian institutions following 2016 elections. The overall macroeconomic framework is considered adequate for the operation's purpose, with a positive medium-term economic outlook projecting growth to reach 5.2 percent in 2016 and average 5.4 percent through 2019, supported by stronger agriculture and a recovering oil sector. However, the fiscal deficit saw a substantial increase from 2.6 to 9.1 percent.

For forecasting overall activity success, several factors are relevant. The macroeconomic framework is assessed as adequate, and the positive medium-term growth outlook is a favorable condition. The government has demonstrated commitment through its PDES, which has been commended by a World Bank-IMF Joint Staff Advisory Note for its comprehensive analysis and realistic plan, and it is actively preparing a successor strategy (EDD). The PIRSC series is well-aligned with these government priorities. Progress has been noted in certain PFM areas, such as an improvement in Niger's Open Budget Index score from 3 in 2010 to 17 in 2015, and in transposing WAEMU PFM directives. Furthermore, the World Bank and IMF are coordinating closely on a new medium-term program (2017-2020) supported by a successor ECF, which focuses on fiscal and debt sustainability, revenue mobilization, and growth. This Second Public Investment Reform Support Credit also builds on the first operation in the series, aiming to achieve key milestones in irrigation and electricity sectors while providing much-needed resources during a challenging fiscal situation. However, significant challenges exist, including Niger's budget transparency remaining considerably below the regional average (17 compared to 45), and considerable delays in applying new WAEMU PFM legislation. The 2016 PEFA report concluded that Niger's PFM system is generally weak, with 20 out of 31 indicators scoring D or D+. Main risks include commodity price shocks, regional security challenges, and institutional/governance bottlenecks, though these are considered manageable. The early closure of the PIRSC series (from three planned operations to two) indicates a recognition of evolving country contexts and emerging challenges that required a re-evaluation of the program. Efforts are specifically needed to address the very low access to electricity (10% countrywide) and the high dependence of rural households on rain-fed agriculture, necessitating efficient operation and regular maintenance of irrigation infrastructure. The operation's ability to navigate these institutional weaknesses, external shocks, and implement reforms effectively will be crucial for its success.

ACTIVITY ID: 44000-P160033

ACTIVITY DESCRIPTION: The Mozambique Forest Investment Project (P160033) is an Investment Project Financing instrument, identified as a Series of Projects, approved on March 7, 2017, and scheduled to close on June 30, 2022. It is categorized as Environmental Assessment Category B - Partial Assessment, and does not involve Bank/IFC collaboration. The Project Development Objective is to improve the practices and enabling environment for forest and land management in Targeted Landscapes. The project's components include Promoting Integrated Landscape Management (US$19.10 million), Strengthening the Enabling Conditions for Sustainable Forest Management (US$20.20 million), and Project Coordination and Management (US$7.70 million). The Ministry of Economy and Finance is the Borrower, while the Ministry of Land, Environment and Rural Development serves as the Implementing Agency.

The project aligns with the Mozambican government's recognized commitment to addressing forest-related challenges, evidenced by the establishment of MITADER, adoption of the National Sustainable Development Program, actions like a participatory audit of forest concessions, suspension of exploration requests, a ban on log exports, and the creation of AQUA and the National Sustainable Development Fund (FNDS). It finances the implementation of Mozambique's national Reducing Emissions from Deforestation and Forest Degradation (REDD+) Program, which aims to reduce deforestation by 40 percent and restore 1 million hectares of forests by 2030, through national-level activities and on-the-ground investments in targeted landscapes in Cabo Delgado and Zambezia provinces. The REDD+ Strategy also emphasizes expanding multipurpose plantation forestry to increase commercial forest plantation areas from 60,000 hectares to over 1 million hectares by 2030, presenting opportunities for rural economic growth through private investments. The project seeks to harness the potential for communities to benefit from forests by promoting systematic community land delimitation, legal registration of land use rights, capacity building, and market linkages.

This project contributes to the World Bank Group's twin goals of ending extreme poverty and boosting shared prosperity by enhancing economic opportunities and improving the well-being of vulnerable rural households, securing land tenure rights, diversifying livelihoods, boosting agricultural productivity, and linking to markets. It also contributes to Mozambique’s Country Partnership Framework (FY17-21) pillars of diversified growth, human capital, and sustainability, by promoting sustainable forest and non-timber resource use, conservation agriculture, and strengthening local communities' management of natural resources. Furthermore, it covers the three areas of intervention of the WB's Forest Action Plan FY16-20, directing investments towards sustainable forest management, value chains, 'forest-smart' interventions, and addressing crosscutting themes of rights, participation, institutions, and governance. The project is structured as a 'Series of Projects' (SoP) designed for scalability, initially targeting high poverty and forestry potential landscapes in nine districts in Zambézia and seven districts in Cabo Delgado, with potential for future geographical expansion upon satisfactory implementation.

The direct beneficiaries include approximately 163,000 households in the targeted districts, including rural communities, women, and youth, who will benefit from land titles, economic opportunities from forest plantation and agroforestry, improved productive inputs, technical assistance, training, and market access. Specifically, about 3,300 small and medium landholders in timber, non-timber forest, charcoal, and agricultural products will receive support in management/business plans, technology training, and market access. Key government institutions at the national level, such as MITADER (including DINAF, AQUA, ANAC, DINOTER) and the Ministry of Agriculture and Food Security (including its National Directorates of Agriculture and Silviculture, and Agriculture and Extension), will receive support for strategic planning, governance improvement, and technical assistance.

For forecasting overall activity success, several factors are critical. The strong government commitment and strategic alignment with national programs like the REDD+ Strategy and the National Sustainable Development Program, coupled with clear objectives for deforestation reduction and forest restoration, suggest a robust strategic foundation. The project's structure as a Series of Projects allows for adaptive scaling, and the detailed identification of direct beneficiaries and specific interventions enhances its potential impact. However, the overall risk rating for the project is substantial. Key risks include political and governance challenges, such as powerful interests benefiting from the status quo, potential resentment from communities, shifts in political appointments, and ongoing conflict in central and northern Mozambique. Macroeconomic risks, including increased debt, currency depreciation, external shocks, and a difficult business environment, could affect investor appetite and project opportunities. Weak governance in the sector, leading to illegality, limited transparency, and a lack of strategic vision, also poses a significant risk. Mitigation strategies involve a broad suite of policy and practice improvements, communication efforts, policy dialogue, and coordination with development partners. The project's success will heavily depend on the effective management and mitigation of these substantial political, macroeconomic, and governance risks, which directly bear on improving the "enabling environment" for forest and land management.

ACTIVITY ID: 44000-P160123

ACTIVITY DESCRIPTION: The State Consolidation Development Program (SCDP) is the first in a programmatic series of two development policy operations, proposed as part of a larger program financed under the IDA Turn-Around Facility. The program's development objective is to support the reestablishment of basic fiscal management and transparency, as well as economic recovery in the Central African Republic (CAR). The Recipient is the Central African Republic, operating through its Ministry of Finance and Budget, and the implementation agency is the Cellule Chargée du Suivi des Réformes Economique et Financières (CS-REF), the Unit Responsible for the Monitoring of Economic and Financial Reforms. The financing for this operation is an IDA Grant of SDR 14.4 million, equivalent to US$20 million, allocated in a single withdrawal tranche. The program is designed to be implemented over a two-year period, implying a progressive scaling-up of reforms.

The SCDP is structured around two main pillars. The first pillar, "Reestablishing Basic Fiscal Management and Transparency," aims to increase fiscal revenues, improve wage bill management, reduce the use of extraordinary non-salary spending, and enhance budget control and transparency. Specific measures under this pillar include supporting the government in increasing revenues by better managing tax and customs exonerations, aligning petroleum prices with international reference prices, improving the integrity and control of the public wage bill, reducing exceptional spending procedures by introducing a nomenclature of supporting documents and a manual of procedures for public spending, and improving budget transparency by regularly publishing budget information, including clearing the backlog of annual Lois de Règlement (Settlement Law of the Finance Law). The second pillar, "Supporting Economic Recovery," seeks to support a pro-poor reform agenda that reinvigorates economic growth drivers in critical sectors such as transport, agriculture, forestry, and telecommunications/Information and Communication Technology (ICT). Actions under this pillar involve supporting critical reforms for the Road Maintenance Fund and the National Equipment Office to ensure more sustainable and efficient allocation of resources for secondary feeder road maintenance, particularly in cotton-producing regions. It also directly supports the agricultural sector by increasing access to high-quality inputs like seeds, fertilizers, and phytosanitary products, and policy measures to initiate production in inoperative, attributed forestry concessions. Furthermore, the program will support the development of the ICT sector by expanding connectivity, mobile telephone penetration, and 2G mobile network coverage across the territory.

The proposed reform areas adopt a selective, gradual, and scalable approach to reengagement in the current post-conflict environment. These reforms are deemed feasible in the short term, laying foundations for addressing long-term governance challenges, improving economic opportunities for all citizens, and targeting the poorest and most remote areas. This focus on the agricultural sector aligns with a pro-poor agenda. The program is closely aligned with the priorities expressed by the Recovery and Peace Building Assessment (RPBA), the overarching framework for CAR's development agenda for 2016-2021, and the Government's General Policy Statement of June 7, 2016. It is also introduced in the context of ongoing Demobilization, Disarmament, and Reintegration (DDR) and Internally Displaced Person (IDP) reinsertion programs, which help reduce community tensions and promote reconciliation. The SCDP has been prepared in parallel with the Mutual Accountability Framework of the United Nations Multidimensional Integrated Stabilization Mission in the Central African Republic (MINUSCA). The International Development Association (IDA) is collaborating closely with the IMF, which prepared a three-year Extended Credit Facility (ECF) program approved in July 2016.

For forecasting overall activity success, several factors are relevant. The CAR economy has shown modest growth, with real GDP increasing by 1.0 percent in 2014 and 4.8 percent in 2015, driven primarily by increased demand and investment, and supply-side recovery led by agriculture and services. Inflation declined to 4.5 percent in 2015, closer to the 3 percent target of the Central African Economic and Monetary Community (CEMAC), although prices have risen since December 2015 due to strong domestic demand and limited food supply. A progressive return to political stability and a peaceful political transition in March 2016 provide a significant opportunity for consolidating peace and economic recovery, with early signs of economic recovery, restored public services, and increased aid flows. However, the overall risk rating for the operation is "Substantial," due to the fragile country context and uncertainty. ACTIVITY DESCRIPTION: The Rural Electrification Project Stage II (P160658) is an Investment Project Financing instrument by The World Bank, approved on May 31, 2017, with a closing date of June 30, 2022. It is classified under Environmental Assessment Category B - Partial Assessment and is not a regionally tagged project. The Project Development Objective is to support increased penetration of renewable energy and increased access to electricity services in the dispersed off-grid areas of Vanuatu. The borrower for this project is the Ministry of Finance and Economic Management, and the implementing agency is the Department of Energy, Ministry of Climate Change and Natural Disaster.

The project consists of three main components: Provision of Solar Home Systems and Micro Grids systems in rural areas of Vanuatu, budgeted at $5,370,000.00; Construction of Mini Grids systems in rural areas of Vanuatu, budgeted at $6,800,000.00; and Technical Assistance and Project Management, budgeted at $2,000,000.00. Mini grids are defined as AC grids connecting households and businesses with SE4ALL Tier 4 or 5 access to electricity supply, capable of receiving generation from sources like mini hydros and biomass, and potentially interconnecting into island grids. The project specifically targets households in off-grid areas that are relatively concentrated and can benefit from advanced Solar Home Systems (SHS) or micro/mini grid configurations, including schools, health centers, dispensaries, post offices, and aid stations, with project design informed by a pre-feasibility study. VREP II will contribute an additional 20 percent of households with access to electricity and aims to increase the share of generation of electricity from renewable sources.

This project is well-aligned with Vanuatu's national energy priorities and broader development goals. The Government of Vanuatu has made electricity sector development a top priority, as outlined in its World Bank-supported Vanuatu National Energy Roadmap (NERM) 2013-2020. The NERM aims for 100 percent access to secure, reliable, and affordable electricity for all citizens by 2030, lower cost energy services, and achieving 65 percent electricity generation from renewable energy by 2020 and 100 percent by 2030. The project supports key NERM targets, including providing modern electricity access to 100 percent of households and public institutions in off-grid areas by 2030, and generating 100 percent of electricity from renewable sources, with 14 percent from biofuels.

For forecasting overall activity success, several factors are noteworthy. The project addresses a critical need, as the lack of modern energy services contributes to "energy poverty" and hinders socio-economic development in rural and low-income populations. Its design is informed by a strategic framework for scaling up electricity access nationwide by 2030, and it is positioned as Stage II of the Vanuatu Rural Electrification Project (VREP), following VREP I which focused on "plug-and-play" solar systems for dispersed off-grid households. The project also fits into the broader World Bank Group's Regional Partnership Framework for Pacific Island Countries and contributes to global efforts to mitigate climate change by promoting clean energy technologies, displacing kerosene use, and supporting the World Bank Group's twin goals of eliminating extreme poverty and boosting shared prosperity, as well as the Sustainable Energy for all (SE4ALL) objectives and the SREP Investment Plan. Furthermore, the project is expected to contribute to increased gender equality and the achievement of Sustainable Development Goals (SDG 5 and SDG 7), emphasizing the positive impact of electricity access on education, health, income generation, and social well-being. The clear objectives, defined components with allocated costs, and strong alignment with national and international development agendas suggest a robust foundation for success.

ACTIVITY DESCRIPTION: "The \"First Fiscal Reform and Growth Credit\" activity, formally known as the First Fiscal Reform and Growth Development Policy Financing (FRG DPF), is a programmatic, single tranche operation for the Republic of Benin, with the Ministry of Economy and Finance (MEF) as the implementing agency. It is supported by an IDA Credit of EUR 33.9 million, equivalent to US$40 million, under standard IDA credit terms including a 38-year maturity and a 6-year grace period. The overall risk rating for the operation is substantial, although no short or long-term climate or disaster risks relevant to the operation have been identified. The program's development objectives are to support macroeconomic stability by strengthening fiscal management and to promote economic growth by increasing agricultural productivity and competitiveness, alongside strengthening the power sector's financial viability and production capacity. These objectives are situated within Benin's broader development framework, including the \"Bénin Révélé\" (Benin Revealed) action plan, the \"Plan National de Développement (PND) 2017-2025,\" and the \"Programme de Croissance pour le Développement Durable (PC2D) 2017-2021,\" which emphasize good governance, climate change mitigation, and youth employment. This activity follows the 2011-2015 Growth and Poverty Reduction Strategy (GPRS), which, despite fostering economic growth, did not prevent a rise in monetary poverty from 37% in 2011 to 40% in 2015, highlighting the need for more effective poverty reduction strategies. Additionally, Benin entered a new three-year IMF extended credit facility program in 2017, which was assessed as satisfactorily implemented as of September 2017. \n\nThe activity is structured around two main pillars with specific targets for 2019. Pillar 1 focuses on strengthening fiscal management. Key targets include increasing tax revenue to 14.5 percent of GDP from a 2014-16 average of 13.9 percent, processing 70 percent of non-custom taxes through banking or e-payment systems (up from 0 percent in 2016), removing 70 percent of identified ghost workers from the civil service payroll (up from 0 percent in 2016), and processing 80 percent of specific payments (pensions above CFAF 50,000, scholarships, and civil servants' benefits) through the banking system (up from 0 percent in 2016). These measures are projected to streamline spending, create fiscal space, and reduce fraud. Pillar 2 targets increasing agricultural productivity and competitiveness, and strengthening the power sector's financial viability and production capacity. For agriculture, the activity aims to improve the average quantity of processed cashew, pineapple juice, and milled rice per ton produced (e.g., cashew processing efficiency from 55 kg/ton to 58 kg/ton), expand irrigated land to 20,600 ha (from 20,000 ha in 2017), and ensure a higher proportion of cashew and pineapple samples meet international standards (e.g., cashew from 90% to 96%). In the power sector, targets include reducing SBEE's (Société Béninoise d'Energie Electrique) arrears with CEB to CFAF 10 billion (from CFAF 39.2 billion in 2017), increasing SBEE's revenue collection rate from public sector institutions to 70 percent (from 5 percent in 2016), and establishing a legal framework supporting private sector participation in the power sector. A Poverty and Social Impact Assessment (PSIA) on electricity pricing reforms indicated that improving SBEE's financial situation is crucial for expanding access, suggesting usage-based tariffs and eliminating fixed costs for small customers as more effective alternatives to current subsidies. The reforms are expected to positively impact poverty reduction by ensuring fiscal space for essential public services, promoting economic growth, and creating private sector employment, particularly by boosting incomes in agriculture and fostering firm creation in the formal sector through reliable and affordable electricity. The supported policies are policy-oriented and are not expected to have negative environmental impacts on the country's natural assets, aligning with the World Bank's cascade approach to attracting private investment."

ACTIVITY DESCRIPTION: The Energy Service Improvement Project (P161015) is an Investment Project Financing initiative by The World Bank, approved on June 23, 2017, and set to close on December 31, 2023. It is classified under Environmental Assessment Category B - Partial Assessment and is not regionally tagged, with the Government of the Republic of Benin acting as the borrower. The project's development objective is to improve SBEE's operational performance, expand electricity access in targeted areas, and promote community-based management of forest resources. This aligns with Benin's FY2013-2017 Country Partnership Strategy, which focuses on sustainable growth, competitiveness, employment, and improved infrastructure services, as well as the World Bank Group's Energy Directions Paper aiming to secure affordable, reliable, and sustainable energy for poverty reduction and shared prosperity.

The project addresses critical challenges within Benin's energy sector. SBEE, the national utility, faces severe financial difficulties, with rising service costs exceeding revenues in 2015, leading to an increasing deficit and a struggle to clear account payables, which escalated from 1.5 years in 2007 to three years in 2015. This situation significantly undermines the financial viability of the power sector. The distribution sector exhibits substantial inefficiencies, including commercial and technical losses totaling about 24 percent. Approximately 40 percent of SBEE's billed amounts remain uncollected, with public facilities and high-revenue customers being major contributors, impacting cash flow. A lack of operating budget prevents SBEE from providing electricity connection kits, resulting in a long list of waiting customers. The network is aging, maintenance is inadequate, sub-stations suffer from defective protection systems, and transformers are overloaded, while illegal and unsafe electricity connections are widespread in high-density peri-urban areas of major urban centers such as Cotonou, Porto-Novo, and Parakou. Electricity access is notably low, reaching only 29 percent of households nationally (below Sub-Saharan Africa's 35 percent average), and only 6 percent in rural areas, compared to 56 percent in urban areas. Service quality is poor, characterized by intermittent supply due to unreliable power imports and frequent breakdowns, as well as low voltage levels that hinder equipment operation. Furthermore, Benin's energy sector is dominated by traditional biomass, which accounts for 49 percent of the country's energy balance. This reliance leads to unsustainable harvesting, accelerating forest cover decline, and fails to benefit local rural populations. The widespread use of inefficient traditional stoves contributes to indoor air pollution, negatively affecting women and children, while liquefied petroleum gas (LPG) adoption is limited by high refill costs, sporadic shortages, restricted distribution, and a lack of consumer awareness.

The project is structured into four components with a total estimated cost of US$60 million. Component 1, "Improvement of SBEE's Commercial Performance," budgeted at US$10 million, aims to enhance SBEE's operational performance, improve revenue collection, and strengthen its corporate image. This involves developing and implementing a Management Improvement Plan (MIP) to protect revenues from high-consuming customers, improve management systems, and build capacity for newly acquired systems. Specific activities include the acquisition and installation of smart meters for high-revenue customers, establishment of a metering control center with associated advanced metering infrastructure, and implementation of management information systems software like an Outage Management System and a Geographic Information System. It also includes updating the customer database, establishing and training a theft detection and inspection crew, and providing technical assistance, including an owner's engineer. Additionally, this component will support SBEE in improving customer relationships by installing a gender-sensitive customer call center to manage complaints and track response times, and by conducting customer satisfaction surveys that incorporate an ESMAP multitier energy access diagnostic and sex-disaggregated data to monitor female-headed households. Component 2, "Distribution Network Strengthening in Targeted Areas," allocated US$40 million, focuses on physical improvements to the network. Component 3, "Community-Based Management of Wood Fuels," with a budget of US$5 million, aims to promote sustainable management of forest resources. Component 4, "Sector Development and Implementation Support," also at US$5 million, provides broader support and capacity building for the energy sector.

The project's primary focus is on enhancing SBEE's operational performance by securing revenues, reducing technical losses through strengthening the low voltage (LV) distribution network, and expanding electricity access as a co-benefit. It will also lay the groundwork for a future energy access project by supporting necessary plans and studies, and maintain the positive momentum in the biomass sector established by previous World Bank-supported projects. The intervention complements efforts by other development partners; its Management Improvement Plan will inform the preparation of a business plan for a management contract supported by the Millennium Challenge Corporation (MCC), and its management information systems will complement the customer management platform financed by AFD. The planned distribution network strengthening specifically targets the LV network, complementing MCC's planned rehabilitation of the medium voltage (MV) network, and will be implemented in peri-urban areas of major urban centers that are distinct from those benefiting from AFD's financing. The project is designed to benefit four main groups: households and small businesses in peri-urban areas of Cotonou, Porto-Novo, Parakou, and Natitingou, who will receive additional daily hours of electricity and improved voltage for motorized equipment. Men and women engaged in community-based forest management practices in targeted rural areas will benefit from revenues and income-generating activities. SBEE itself will experience increased revenues from a revenue protection program and the regularization of informal electricity users. Finally, key power sector stakeholders, including MEEM, ABERME, ARE, and ANADER, will benefit from technical assistance and capacity-building activities.

The success of the project's development objectives will be measured by several key PDO-level results indicators. These include a reduction of losses (measured as a percentage), an increase in revenues from targeted high-consuming customers (measured as a percentage), and the number of people provided with new or improved electricity service, which is a Corporate Results Indicator. The integration of gender-sensitive approaches, such as in the design and staffing of the customer call center and the collection of sex-disaggregated data for monitoring female-headed households, reflects a commitment to equitable service improvement.
