ACTIVITY DESCRIPTION: The "Volunteering for Development (V4D)" activity, with a project name of "Volunteering for Development (V4D)", targets multiple countries or regions. Initially, the project had an original budget of £50,000,000. This budget underwent several increases: to £54,500,000 on 20 March 2020, to £56,500,000 on 31 July 2020, to £61,500,000 on 1 October 2020, and finally to £63,000,000 on 31 March 2021. The activity received a cost extension valued at £7,500,000, bringing the total UK budget support to VSO for this program to £70.5 million. The original project start and end dates were 31 March 2017 – 31 March 2020. These dates were subsequently extended multiple times: to 31 July 2020 on 20 March 2020, to 30 September 2020 on 31 July 2020, to 31 March 2021 on 1 October 2020, and to 31 May 2021 on 31 March 2021. The new project end date is 31 March 2022.

The V4D grant supports the UK Government's ambitions on poverty reduction for the 'bottom billion' by using British and international expertise to improve basic services and livelihoods for vulnerable and extremely poor people, building their resilience to shocks and stresses. It employs a community-led development approach that empowers the poorest to participate in decisions and hold development actors and governments accountable. The grant leverages VSO's experience in deploying volunteers to tackle poverty and inequality and is a key part of the UK's international development brand. The original grant aimed to provide targeted support over three years (2017–2020) to enable VSO to drive change across the volunteering for development sector. The activity has enhanced volunteer impact and strengthened Britain's reputation, delivering improved health, education, and livelihoods outcomes for over 4 million marginalized people across 24 developing countries. This is achieved by enhancing practices and effectiveness for at least 350 partner organizations and deploying over 4,000 expert volunteers, mobilizing a global volunteer alumni network of over 5,000 to champion volunteering for SDGs and UK Aid, and promoting widespread adoption of global minimum volunteer standards in the unregulated volunteering sector.

The program's extensions, particularly those in 2020, adapted it to respond to the COVID-19 pandemic and meet the changing needs of an additional 1.5 million beneficiaries. This ensured vulnerable groups could access COVID-19 services through responsive health, education, and livelihood interventions. The blended volunteering model, involving international, national, and community volunteers, has proven effective in maintaining programming despite travel restrictions. The current £7.5 million extension will fund V4D from 1 June 2021 to 31 March 2022, supporting at least five of the Foreign Secretary's seven core priorities, including poverty reduction, climate and biodiversity, COVID-19 and global health security, girls' education, defending open societies, resolving conflict, and humanitarian assistance. VSO's work in strengthening open societies will continue by focusing on building resilient, inclusive, and accountable communities, and by enhancing the capacity of governments, civil society organizations, and service providers to be accountable to marginalized people.

The additional £7.5 million will support V4D in delivering safe, resilient, responsive health, education, and livelihood services, and mitigating COVID-19 effects for poor and marginalized people in 10 developing countries. Planned activities for this period include ensuring continued access to education during COVID-19 (e.g., teacher training, VSO Schools App, post-COVID return-to-school campaigns), empowering beneficiaries to safeguard health (COVID-19 response outreach, health workforce capacity building), supporting fragile contexts through peaceful communities and disaster preparedness, restoring and improving livelihoods (e.g., creating decent employment for marginalized youth, COVID-19 emergency food/cash transfers), and ongoing training of volunteers and partners in resilience, social inclusion, and social accountability. The activity also focuses on global partnerships and uptake of Global Standards through engagement at country and global levels (COVID-19 policy influencing, promotion of Global Standards), sustaining Youth Networks, and enhancing youth leadership in SDGs/VNRs for 2021.

The cost extension will enable V4D to deliver against three outputs: a global program on social inclusion, social accountability, and resilience to extend services to the poorest, strengthen accountability through local ownership and citizen-led accountability; global partnerships and social accountability brokered by VSO to improve service quality; and global uptake of standards for responsible volunteering towards SDGs. The focus will be primarily on output 1, with reduced work for outputs 2 and 3 due to funding reductions. This amendment directly contributes to reducing gender inequality by addressing existing educational, health, and livelihood disparities. All VSO programs will be underpinned by rigorous social exclusion and gender analysis using disaggregated data, and the FCDO will monitor VSO's gender equality delivery, with VSO's 'People First' framework ensuring gender considerations in all policies and practices.

The original approach and theory of change remain valid, though an additional Volunteering for Development Framework model has been introduced to inform future phases and support the current theory of change. VSO has refined its monitoring and reporting tools, measurement methods for scale and reach, volunteer surveys, and partner capacity assessments based on country feedback. VSO's programs pilot and develop the MILE (Measuring Impact for Learning and Empowerment) methodology, an inclusive, beneficiary-focused, and participatory M&E process, with training completed in ten countries, enhancing engagement with beneficiaries and stakeholders. An independent Mid Term Evaluation in 2019 found that integrating core approaches enabled VSO to adopt a more systems-based approach. A final evaluation in August 2020 assessed the contribution of global leadership, global engagement, and the theory of change to the program's impact. This evaluation recognized VSO's good progress in implementing the volunteering for development model and its thought leader role, finding that the development of the Global Standard for responsible impactful volunteering fostered coherence in the sector. It also highlighted strong examples of VSO's contributions and impact on health, livelihoods, and education for marginalized primary actors, good evidence of the effectiveness of the volunteering for development approach for SDGs, and strong examples of primary actors, especially youth, holding duty-bearers accountable and countries utilizing the volunteering for development model.

ACTIVITY DESCRIPTION: The "Responding to Protracted Crisis in Sudan: Humanitarian Reform, Assistance & Resilience Programme (HRAR)" is a bridge funding cost extension activity targeting Sudan, originally spanning from 24/07/2017 to 31/03/2022 with an initial budget of £175 million, which was increased to £235 million in July 2019. This current extension adds £20 million, bringing the total program value to a maximum of £255 million over five years and extending the end date to 30/06/2022, allowing for a potential spend of up to £35.1 million in FY 21/22. The programme's purpose is to deliver urgently needed, life-saving support to internally displaced people, refugees, and disaster-affected communities, while simultaneously strengthening systems to provide earlier and more effective assistance, facilitate recovery, and build resilience. Sudan faces the fifth worst humanitarian crisis globally, with 13.4 million people requiring assistance in 2021, an increase of 36% since 2020, driven by economic crisis, conflict, COVID-19, flooding, and a new refugee crisis. The program aims to provide humanitarian assistance to a minimum of 700,000 people and build the resilience of 38,000 vulnerable households in conflict-affected areas in Darfur during the 21/22 financial year.

The program has three core pillars with specific objectives: System strengthening and reform, focused on enabling more effective humanitarian responses and resilience planning by the Government of Sudan and the UN system through technical assistance (TA) for improved coordination, strategic planning, risk management, and evidence-based decision-making; Response to humanitarian needs, which provides life-saving support for internally displaced people, refugees, and disaster-affected communities, prioritizing preparedness and early action for extreme hunger, displacement, health emergencies, and predictable shocks; and Building resilience, aimed at economic recovery, strengthening household resilience to climatic and conflict-related shocks, and improving access to essential services, particularly in Darfur. Indicative results include providing TA to the World Health Organisation for COVID-19 and health emergency response, supporting the Government of Sudan's emergency response capabilities, rolling out a conflict sensitivity facility, and delivering the FCDO's Special Envoy for Famine Prevention's Protection and Access Plan. Anticipated allocations for humanitarian response in 2021/22 include £6.75 million to WFP to support 370,000 people with food aid, cash, and vouchers; £8 million to the UN-led Sudan Humanitarian Fund (SHF) for 190,000 people's basic healthcare, 44,000 people's food aid, and 44,000 women and children's nutritional support; and £2 million to UNHCR for 10,000 refugees' primary healthcare, 30,000 people's safe water/sanitation, and 20,000 children's primary education. Up to £3 million will be directed to the ‘Taadoud’ project to provide 32,000 households with improved access to natural resources, 5,000 households with improved access to savings and financial services, and assist 1,000 farmers in productive partnerships.

Implementation involves working with existing UN partners (WFP, UNHCR, and SHF) and three international organizations (ICRC, Danish Refugee Council, and an NGO consortium led by Catholic Relief Services), with due diligence undertaken for any new partners. The program has built-in flexibility to react to changing needs or shocks. All partners disaggregate data to ensure marginalized groups, particularly women and children, are targeted and benefit, with gender considerations mainstreamed across activities, marked as 'significant' for Gender Equality. Key risks include persistent economic fragility, high inflation, and currency depreciation, exacerbated by local conflicts, displacement, natural shocks like floods, droughts, and locusts, and a weak health system struggling with COVID-19. Mitigation strategies involve monitoring and adapting the program in discussion with partners, while addressing root causes through a wider portfolio. The 2020 Annual Review noted strong Value for Money (VfM) with partners meeting or exceeding beneficiary targets, demonstrating good procurement practices, and fostering strong supplier relationships. The program's Theory of Change has been updated in response to escalating humanitarian needs, rising food insecurity, increased displacement, a fragile peace process, and heightened security concerns, leading to new activities focusing on additional TA, civilian protection, livelihood opportunities, and operational research. A third-party monitoring mechanism will be piloted this year.

ACTIVITY ID: GB-GOV-1-300303

ACTIVITY DESCRIPTION: The Rural Electrification in Sierra Leone (RESL) programme, implemented by the United Nations Office for Project Services (UNOPS), aims to provide clean, affordable, and reliable energy to rural towns and villages lacking electricity. Originally spanning from July 2016 to November 2021 with a budget of £34,500,000, the project anticipates benefiting an estimated 340,000 people by 2021 through improved income, assets, health, educational outcomes, and reduced CO2 emissions, helping Sierra Leone transition towards renewable energy. An addendum to the business case has introduced a cost and time extension, adding £3,200,000 to the budget, bringing the total commitment to £37.7 million, and extending the new project end date to May 31, 2022. The objective of this extension is to fund non-generation assets and public reserve account payments for the first four years, ensuring tariff stability, affordability, and reducing political interference risk, while also allowing more time for 'productive use' interventions and enhancing the ability to attribute project impact through a longer evaluation period.

The project is structured around several work packages (WP). WP1 successfully installed solar power in 54 Community Health Centres (CHCs) by July 2017, converting 50 of them into solar mini-grids by 2018, which are operated and maintained by three international companies: PowerGen, Power Leone, and Winch, selected via international tender. WP2 aims to complete 44 larger mini-grids by 2021 through a blend of private sector investment for power generation and project grant financing for distribution. WP3 focuses on collaborating with relevant ministries, particularly the Ministry of Energy (MoE), to establish necessary policies and regulations for mini-grids and provides technical assistance for project sustainability. WP4 provided emergency earthworks in Freetown, completed by March 2018, while WP5 handles monitoring, evaluation, and communications. WP6 utilizes targeted matching grants and technical assistance to address constraints and promote the 'productive use' of electricity by local firms.

By March 2020, RESL demonstrated significant achievements, including being recognized as a 'pacesetter' in the African renewable energy sector at the UK Africa Invest Summit 2020. Public-Private Partnership (PPP) agreements were signed with the Government of Sierra Leone (GoSL) following an international tender with the three operators. All 54 CHCs are receiving free electricity, with 50 converted to mini-grids, and 34 of these have completed indoor connections to houses and businesses, with the remaining expected to be operational by May 2020. A presidential 'Lights On' commissioning event was held in December 2019 for all WP1 sites. For WP2 sites, six distribution networks are complete, with nine more in progress, and PowerGen, responsible for half the sites, secured approximately $7 million from InfraCo for procuring panels and batteries. An innovative 'productive use' strategy has led to a call for proposals, attracting around 45 applicants who will receive grants and technical assistance starting in June. Furthermore, the baseline for the project's robust academic difference-in-difference impact evaluation has been completed for both WP1 and WP2 villages.

The additional UK support, totaling £3.2 million, will cover costs for WP2 indoor connection materials (£690,758), WP2 meters (£1,245,191), WP1 reserve account payments (£972,935), and administrative fees (£291,096). This subsidy aims to keep tariffs roughly constant for the first three years, preventing an expected 50% increase in WP2 tariffs due to operators' significant development costs, and ultimately lowering them from the current $0.85/kWh. The project expects an average tariff of $0.65/kWh from June 2020, facilitated by DFID's subsidy and the adoption of a Multi-Year Tariff Order (MYTO) model, which will provide operators long-term security. The time extension will enhance productive use interventions and improve impact evaluation. The project's Value for Money assessment, based on fossil fuel reduction and avoided kerosene costs, yields a benefit-cost ratio of 1.74, and the overall programme benefit-cost ratio is expected to remain positive. A lower tariff is anticipated to increase electricity demand by approximately 30% for both households and businesses, fostering higher usage, more productive activities, job creation, and improved health and educational outcomes.

Implementation will involve amending the existing Memorandum of Understanding with UNOPS, which will manage a new WP7 'Tariff adjustment fund management' to disburse funds for connection materials and reserve account payments. The programme is fully compliant with the International Development (Gender Equality) Act 2014, with specific measures in WP6 to ensure women's participation in matching grants through reduced co-contributions, prioritization of women-owned businesses, and referrals for training, with a target that 50% of training beneficiaries are women. The MoE has shown enthusiasm for the cost extension and the MYTO proposal.

ACTIVITY DESCRIPTION: The Somaliland Development Fund Phase Two (SDF2), a multi-donor fund targeting Somaliland, serves as a flagship economic recovery and infrastructure programme supporting the Government of Somaliland (GoSL) in delivering basic services. Active from September 2018, its original end date was March 2024. The programme coordinates donor investment for critical infrastructure projects aligned with the second Somaliland National Development Plan (NDP2) 2017-2021. Its objectives include fostering inclusive economic growth through strategic infrastructure investment, strengthening GoSL's capability to manage sustainable infrastructure development, supporting strong government ownership of development priorities, and enhancing citizen perception of GoSL through improved service delivery.

The original project budget was £25 million. An addendum to the business case seeks to increase its overall value from £25 million to £61.9 million, primarily reflecting additional contributions from other donors, and without requiring new funding from the UK. The UK, through the Foreign, Commonwealth and Development Office (FCDO), is the lead donor, with an original commitment of £25 million. The fund has successfully leveraged significant funding from other partners, including Denmark (£21.6m), Netherlands (£13.7m), and Norway (£2.5m). These partner contributions were crucial in sustaining programme delivery between 2020 and 2023 when the UK paused further contributions due to ODA spending cuts, freezing its commitment at £10.46 million. The addendum also approves a time extension of one additional year, shifting the project end date to March 2025. This extension will absorb £13.4 million of Capital Departmental Expenditure Limit (CDEL) spend for FY 2024/25 within the UK's original £25 million commitment, bringing the total UK contribution to £23.24 million and other partner contributions to £37.76 million, for a total business case value of £61.9 million. The Business Case Quest Document Number is 4146898.

The cost and time extension will enable the implementation of two key infrastructure projects. Firstly, it will facilitate the rehabilitation of 70km of the Burao-Berbera Road in eastern Somaliland, connecting the major livestock market in Burao with the seaport of Berbera, and extending connectivity to Hargeisa and Ethiopia via the Berbera corridor. This project, which will complete the rehabilitation of the 132km road, is expected to benefit approximately 50,000 beneficiaries per year by ensuring reliable and affordable market access for Eastern regions, known for livestock, agriculture, and fisheries. Secondly, a Borama water expansion project will be undertaken, involving the drilling and equipping of three additional boreholes in the Western aquifer (Afraaga), their connection to the existing network via a booster station, and the construction of a 500m³ water storage tank at the booster station, powered by a solar system to reduce carbon emissions from diesel generators. This water supply expansion is projected to benefit an additional 45,000 residents of Borama town, Awdal region, by improving access to clean, affordable, and environmentally safe drinking water, thereby reducing waterborne diseases and enhancing health outcomes, particularly for children under five.

The SDF2 is currently managed by Mott McDonald (MM), a private sector agent whose contract was secured through an OJEU tender process and runs until March 2024. Given MM's strong performance and the one-year extension period, it is considered rational and cost-effective to extend their contract rather than undertake a new, competitive tendering process, which would incur significant transaction costs and time delays without providing additional value for money. This extension is consistent with the original business case's strategy and implementation arrangements, with the log frame (FLAG A) being revised to reflect the increased results from the extension of these two existing projects.

Information useful for forecasting overall activity success includes the programme's multi-donor nature, which provides financial resilience, as evidenced by continued delivery despite UK funding pauses. The strong commitment and financial contributions from other donors have been crucial. The established and well-performing managing agent, Mott McDonald, is another positive factor, as their contract extension avoids the risks and costs associated with a new procurement. The focus on completing and expanding existing, high-impact infrastructure projects (road rehabilitation and water supply expansion) with clear beneficiary numbers and expected outcomes also points to a high likelihood of success. The alignment of projects with the Somaliland National Development Plan and strong government ownership further strengthen the programme's foundation. While there have been past delays and uncertainties regarding UK funding, the current addendum addresses these by securing the UK's original commitment and enabling project continuation with a clear new end date and budget.

ACTIVITY ID: GB-GOV-1-300705

ACTIVITY DESCRIPTION: The activity, titled "Delivering ambition of the United Nations Secretary General’s Climate Summit 2019 to build resilience to climate change," targets a global scope with an original budget of £6,400,000. It commenced on 01/11/18 and was originally set to conclude on 31/03/20. The project has undergone a no-cost extension, pushing its new end date to 31/03/2021, with no additional funds provided beyond the existing £6,400,000. The project's purpose is to support the UK's leadership in delivering the Resilience Strand of the UN Secretary General's Climate Summit in September 2019, aiding key partners and UK objectives pre-Summit, and ensuring continued progress on agreed commitments post-September 2019. This no-cost extension updates the business case based on the Summit's latest direction and re-assesses how to maximize impact, incorporating changes such as amending delivery routes for increased oversight of implementing partners, extending the timeline to improve post-Summit outcomes, and including external and independent policy support. The project is identified by Business Case Quest Document No. 41021442 and Project number 300705.

The total £6,400,000 budget is allocated across several components and partners: Global Commission on Adaptation (£2,780,596), Global Resilience Partnership (£499,235), World Economic Forum (£1,864,680), Events (£1,020,655), Secondment and support to the Executive Office of the Secretary General (£150,000), and Policy support and inward secondments (£100,000). The expected results aim for the Summit to drive a transformational change, enabling global adaptation and resilience to climate shocks and stresses, fundamentally altering decision-making for food, water, and jobs. Key deliverables include a political declaration for a headline shift, systemic transformation, capacity building, and high-profile initiatives demonstrating practical action. The anticipated impact is a global shift in resilience approaches by countries, the private sector, and multilaterals to help the world's poorest. The outcome expects public and private investors to mainstream climate risk management into their investment decisions. Outputs include developing and sharing evidence, analysis, and recommendations; delivering high-level events and products before and after the Summit to engage stakeholders; and securing pledges from influential actors to implement Summit actions.

Programme management involves a dedicated programme manager and an A1 Senior Responsible Owner overseeing fund utilisation according to Smart Rules, including monitoring partner relationships. Risk management is robust, with a programme-level risk register and a separate Summit-specific register, both reviewed quarterly and escalated. Each implementing partner also maintains a risk register, reviewed monthly by DFID and the partner. DFID ensures frequent communication, monthly progress and risk reviews, and receives formal quarterly financial and narrative reports, with key risks escalated up the departmental and directorate risk registers.

Key changes from the original business case include a one-year extension of the programme end date to 31/03/2021 to ensure sustainability and accountability of Summit commitments, aligning with the UK's bid to host COP 26 and supporting the Global Commission on Adaptation's 'Action Track' process. This extension is designed to maximize UK influence, impact, and Summit sustainability. The budget for UK events has been reduced, now covering UK-hosted events and travel for senior LDC representatives. Funds have been reallocated to support a G77 representative's secondment to the UN Summit team, ensuring a Southern perspective. Significantly, DFID will now hold a direct partnership with the Global Resilience Partnership (GRP) instead of it being a downstream partner of the World Economic Forum, aiming for greater oversight, maximum impact, and reduced delivery chain inefficiencies. A new budget line has been created to fund policy advice from expert organizations, bringing in external expertise for UK approach development. Financial amendments also include quarterly estimates for the current and next financial year, noting RDEL costs are subject to annual allocations, and confirming all funds remain ODA eligible. These changes, particularly the direct partnership with GRP and dedicated policy support, are intended to strengthen oversight and enhance the programme's overall effectiveness and success.

ACTIVITY ID: XM-DAC-46004-40156-033-LN3549

ACTIVITY DESCRIPTION: The Sustainable Coastal Protection and Management Investment Program - Tranche 2 (Project 2) is the second tranche of a $250.0 million multitranche financing facility approved by the Asian Development Bank (ADB) on September 29, 2010, for the coastal states of Goa (later excluded), Karnataka, and Maharashtra. This overarching investment program, the SCPMIP, is being implemented over 10 years and aims to address immediate coastal protection needs and instability using environmentally and socially appropriate structural solutions, with a focus on softer options like artificial reefs, beach nourishment, and dune management. The program finances the development of shoreline management plans (SMPs), information systems, and institutional capacities to meet long-term needs, introducing new technologies to protect the coastline and enhance income-generating opportunities for coastal communities. Progressing coastline erosion is a significant threat in these states, intensified by rising sea levels and more frequent/intense storms, with severe economic and environmental consequences.

ADB received the periodic financing request for Tranche 2 from the Government of India on May 26, 2016, and it is included in India's country operations business plan for 2016–2018. Project 2 will specifically focus on Karnataka, where coastal erosion poses a high risk, and will protect a total of 54 kilometers of coastline in the three selected coastal districts: Dakshina Kannada, Udupi, and Uttara Kannada. It is funded with $93.54 million, comprising $65.50 million from ADB and $28.04 million from the Government of Karnataka. Project 2 will support nine subprojects, consisting of six coastal protection subprojects designed for medium to severe coastal erosion and three community subprojects for areas of low erosion. Its main activities include constructing and upgrading coastal erosion and instability mitigation structures, and capacity building for integrated shoreline planning and development in Karnataka.

Key capacity building and institutional development activities for Project 2 encompass updating shoreline management plans to include climate change impacts and expanded stakeholder consultation, updating the coastal management information system (CMIS), implementing a comprehensive near-shore seabed sand resources analysis study, conducting numerical modeling of coastal sections for climate change impact assessments, preparing detailed designs for a community protection subproject (beach nourishment, dune reconstruction, planting), and adjusting designs for other subprojects based on actual beach profiles. Furthermore, it will strengthen the Coastal Infrastructure Management Unit (CIMU)—established under Tranche 1 in May 2015—as a hub for long-term sustainable planning, support the establishment and training of Shoreline Management Organizations (SMOs) at nine subproject sites, and enhance the capacities of stakeholders and the executing agency in project management, finance, construction, and community participation. The CIMU will also coordinate various agencies, support long-term coastal sustainability, manage CMIS data, and ensure SMO functioning.

The subproject designs incorporate international standards for sustainable coastal protection, focusing on "soft approaches" and new initiatives not commonly used in India, such as sand nourishment with groynes (sourced from surplus beach areas) and new revetment approaches using geotextile sand-filled bags or re-designed rock revetments with low front slopes. These solutions aim to minimize impact on natural coastal processes and increase sand volume. The anticipated impact of Project 2 is improved income and poverty status for coastal communities in Karnataka, aligned with the National Flood Management Program, with the outcome being protected and managed shorelines in Karnataka.

For forecasting overall success, the preceding Tranche 1 demonstrated satisfactory performance, with the Project Management Unit (PMU) capacity deemed satisfactory, most loan covenants complied with or being complied with, no social safeguards impacts or grievances, and environmental monitoring reports regularly submitted. While private sector participation in coastal protection and management showed limited interest in Tranche 1, it did not affect project performance. The strong emphasis on long-term institutional strengthening through CIMU and SMOs, comprehensive planning via updated SMPs and CMIS, and direct stakeholder engagement indicate a sustainable approach. The adoption of environmentally and socially appropriate solutions, including new technologies and "soft approaches," suggests a commitment to effective and resilient coastal management. The clear focus on addressing a well-defined and significant problem (coastal erosion exacerbated by climate change) with a structured investment program provides a solid foundation for success.

ACTIVITY DESCRIPTION: The Southeast Gobi Urban and Border Town Development Project is an additional financing initiative with a total estimated cost of $21.75 million, comprising a $20.00 million regular loan from the Asian Development Bank (ADB) and $1.75 million in counterpart funding from the Government of Mongolia, which is also the borrower. The project, with a number of 42184-027, falls under the Water and other urban infrastructure and services sector, specifically urban sewerage, and is managed by the EARD/EASS department/division with the Ministry of Construction and Urban Development as the executing agency. The activity is classified with a low climate change impact, effective gender mainstreaming, and a high urban location impact. Its strategic agenda includes inclusive economic growth, focusing on access to more inclusive economic opportunities and jobs, and environmentally sustainable growth through urban environmental improvement. The project targets SDG6 and SDG9, but not geographically or household-specifically. Risk categorization is low, with Environment and Involuntary Resettlement categorized as B, and Indigenous Peoples as C.

This proposed loan represents a second additional financing building upon a successful original grant and a first additional financing loan. The initial grant of $15.0 million, approved in April 2010 for urban infrastructure and service improvements in the Southeast Gobi, was implemented on time and completed by July 2018 with 98% physical progress and disbursement. The first additional loan of $19.43 million, approved in April 2016 for wastewater treatment plants (WWTPs) in four aimag centers (Arvaikheer, Dalanzadgad, Sainshand, Tsetserleg), is currently on track with 19.7% disbursement and 58.3% contract awards by July 2018, and is complemented by a $1.0 million technical assistance for sludge reuse. This proposed second additional financing aims to scale up and extend the socioeconomic benefits by improving wastewater management services in five additional aimag centers: Baruun-Urt (Sukhbaatar province), Bulgan City (Bulgan province), Chinggis City (Khentii province), Mandalgovi (Dundgovi province), and Murun (Khuvsgul province). These rapidly growing second-tier cities collectively had an urban population of about 115,000 in 2017, and two of them (Chinggis City and Baruun-Urt) have shown above-average population and GDP increases, while the others also contribute significantly. The project is expected to benefit an estimated 30,273 households, businesses, and institutions directly.

The proposed project's impact aligns with enhancing economic development and livability in aimag centers, with outcomes focused on improved urban governance and expanded access to sustainable urban infrastructure and services. It will deliver two main outputs: (i) constructing and operationalizing wastewater collection and treatment infrastructure, specifically modern WWTPs with an individual operating capacity of 3,000 m³ in the five new aimag centers to replace inadequate waste stabilization pond systems and deliver effluent meeting Mongolian standards, alongside providing vacuum evacuation trucks for ger area sanitation; and (ii) strengthening strategic planning, management, and cost recovery for wastewater management through expert support for various aspects like utility management, emergency preparedness, and construction supervision, as well as strengthening PMU and PUSO capacities, providing training, study tours, policy dialogue on tariff reform, and public awareness campaigns. Project design incorporates lessons learned from the current project, including the use of energy-efficient integrated fixed-film activated sludge (IFAS) technology, proven effective in cold climates and already working well under the first additional financing, which was selected after a two-stage evaluation for its operational efficiency, cost-effectiveness, and robustness. The existing Project Management Unit's advanced implementation capacity and experience with similar technical, economic, financial, and safeguard requirements further support effective administration and risk mitigation for this additional financing.

ACTIVITY DESCRIPTION: "The Urban Environment and Climate Change Adaptation Project proposes a loan and grant to the Socialist Republic of Viet Nam to improve urban environmental conditions and adapt to climate change in Dong Hoi and Hoi An cities. The total estimated cost is $144.16 million, including $100 million from the Asian Development Bank (ADB), a $4 million grant from the Urban Climate Change Resilience Trust Fund (UCCRTF) under the Urban Financing Partnership Facility, and a $40.16 million contribution from the Government of Viet Nam. The project addresses critical urban environmental issues in Viet Nam, such as inadequate infrastructure investment, limited local government capacity for urban services, poor operation and maintenance, and unmanaged waste disposal, which render cities less competitive and livable. It also tackles significant climate change challenges, including historical sea level rise (20 centimeters since the mid-1960s) and temperature increases (0.5-0.7 degrees), with projections indicating further rises of 75-100 centimeters in sea level and 2-3 degrees Celsius in temperature by the century's end, threatening 10-12% of the population and 10% of GDP. The project supports the development of secondary cities like Dong Hoi and Hoi An, chosen for being disaster-prone coastal areas; Dong Hoi, with a 2010 population of 113,900 and 1.8% annual growth, has a history of infrastructure destruction, while Hoi An, with 92,000 inhabitants and 2.5% annual growth, faces unsustainable tourism growth and environmental impacts. The project aligns with Viet Nam's Socio-Economic Development Plan and ADB's strategies, prioritizing environmental protection, disaster prevention, climate change adaptation, and strengthening secondary cities. To improve outcomes, lessons from previous urban development projects in Viet Nam have been incorporated into the design, including emphasizing advance actions, tariff reform for financial sustainability, and promoting provincial government ownership. Special features include climate-proofing infrastructure designs, complementary structural and nonstructural measures like flood forecasting and warning systems, and a fiscal analysis of the project provinces. The ADB loan funds are relended from the central government to provincial entities—Quang Binh Provincial People's Committee (PPC), Quang Nam PPC, and Quang Nam Water Supply and Drainage Joint Stock Company (WSDC)—which will bear foreign exchange and interest rate variation risks, a crucial factor for forecasting the project's long-term financial stability. The expected impact is improved urban environments in Dong Hoi and Hoi An, resulting in greater access to improved urban services. Project outputs include new and improved climate change-resilient urban infrastructure in Dong Hoi (Bao Ninh urban development and wastewater management complementing a World Bank-funded project) and Hoi An (wastewater treatment plant, Co Co urban development, integrated flood management, and water supply improvements), along with enhanced competencies in urban environmental management and climate change adaptation through implementation support, capacity development, and awareness programs for residents. The Quang Binh PPC and Quang Nam PPC, both experienced in managing development partner-funded projects, are the executing agencies, with Quang Binh Urban Environment Company (URENCO) and Quang Nam WSDC as implementing agencies. Two Project Management Units (PMUs) have been established, and the project is scheduled for implementation from December 2015 to December 2022, with a loan and grant closing date of June 30, 2023. No independent oversight body will be established for the project."

Output 2 focuses on capacity building for effective contract awarding and timely completion, including training 10-15 key staff (at least 30% women) in areas such as project planning, design, construction, asset management, O&M, road safety, and traffic management. The total estimated cost for the project is $5.11 million."

ACTIVITY ID: XM-DAC-46004-47047-002-LN3400

ACTIVITY DESCRIPTION: "The Shandong Groundwater Protection Project aims to provide a sustained groundwater supply for the agriculture sector in the Weifang-Zibo area, ultimately improving the conservation and sustainable use of groundwater resources in these prefectures. This initiative is designed as a demonstration of integrated approaches for environmental restoration of overexploited groundwater aquifers through enhanced water resource allocation, improved storm water retention, and rehabilitation of the water environment, thereby protecting groundwater quantity and quality and contributing to food security. The project's rationale stems from the PRC's heavy reliance on groundwater, particularly in the northern regions where agriculture accounts for 60% of its use, leading to over 100 large cones of groundwater depression, with 70,000 square kilometers in the North China Plain. The Weifang-Zibo area, a significant vegetable producer, has the largest overexploited aquifer in Shandong Province, covering approximately 5,422 square kilometers, due to extensive groundwater abstraction, particularly for shallow groundwater sources used by greenhouses. Current groundwater recharge is insufficient, and seasonal rainfall patterns, which often lead to unutilized storm waters flowing to the sea, are expected to worsen.

By 2023, the project targets an average groundwater level recovery over 120,000 hectares in the groundwater depression zone and an additional 60 million m³ of surface water retained. Specific outputs by 2022 include the creation of about 880 hectares of wetlands, an increase in annual water supply to Mata Lake and Judian Lake wetlands to 47 million m³ (from a 2014 baseline of 12 million m³), and the operationalization of a water resources monitoring system in Huantai County. Output 1, focused on groundwater recharge and conservation, will introduce groundwater recharge technologies and involve rehabilitating/constructing wetland areas in Huantai County and Shouguang City, alongside developing a comprehensive management information system for water resources monitoring in Huantai County. Output 2, aimed at improving and monitoring surface water allocation, includes constructing, rehabilitating, and improving about 345 km of canals and enhancing the capacity of the Nanzhai and Juchenghe reservoirs to retain and store up to 8.3 million m³ of rainwater per year (from a 2014 baseline of 3.7 million m³). Output 3 will enhance capacity development for water resources management, implementing demonstration activities in Changle, Huantai, Gaomi, Qingzhou, and Shouguang, including groundwater recharge through water harvesting in smart greenhouses, comprehensive water management information systems, and capacity building in water resource policy, regulation, pricing, and markets.

Potential risks that could affect the project's success and final outcome include inadequate institutional coordination among local governments regarding improved water resource allocation, increasing water demand from urban and industrial sectors to unsustainable levels, late release of counterpart funds, and potential problems or delays encountered during the implementation of resettlement plans. These risks highlight challenges that could impact the project's ability to achieve its targets for groundwater recovery and sustainable water management."

ACTIVITY ID: XM-DAC-46004-48434-003-GRNT0495

ACTIVITY DESCRIPTION: The Visakhapatnam-Chennai Industrial Corridor Development Program, Project 1 (VCICDP), is an investment program in India, specifically within Andhra Pradesh, with a project number of 48434-002 / 48434-005. Executed by the Department of Industries & Commerce, Government of Andhra Pradesh, it is a multi-tranche financing facility (MFF) combined with a policy-based loan (PBL). The program's total cost is $720.00 million, comprising an ADB MFF loan of $500.00 million (covering large and medium industries, electricity transmission and distribution, road transport, and urban water supply), an ADB PBL of $126.00 million (including a $1.00 million technical assistance special fund and a $125.00 million program loan), cofinancing of $5.00 million from the Urban Climate Change Resilience Trust Fund (a grant), and a government counterpart contribution of $215.00 million.

The VCICDP is strategically aligned with inclusive economic growth by fostering access to economic opportunities and jobs, environmentally sustainable growth through addressing global and regional transboundary environmental concerns and urban environmental improvement, and regional integration by enhancing cross-border infrastructure, trade, and investment. It aims to increase the manufacturing sector's contribution to Andhra Pradesh's GDP, trade, and employment, ultimately enhancing the VCIC's growth and competitiveness. Key outputs include strengthening corridor management and ease of doing business, making VCIC infrastructure more resilient, and enhancing institutional capacities, human resources, and program management. The policy loan specifically supports capacity development, improving the ease of doing business (including an e-portal and single-desk system with incentives for women entrepreneurs), stimulating industrial development with special incentives for women, and improving investor promotion, trade facilitation, and logistics.

The program's design incorporates effective gender mainstreaming, with specific provisions like incentives for women entrepreneurs. It is categorized as "Complex" in terms of risk and includes Environment (B), Involuntary Resettlement (A), and Indigenous Peoples (C) safeguards for Tranche 1, with a high impact related to Climate Change and Urban location. Project readiness is high, as the government has prepared detailed project reports and bidding documents, with advance contracting planned. The estimated approval for the MFF and Tranche I was 20 September 2016, with completion by 31 December 2024 and 31 December 2022 respectively, while the PBL was also approved on 20 September 2016 for completion by 31 December 2019. The program uses country procurement and public financial management systems, indicating effective development cooperation. The VCIC, covering over 800 km of coastline in Andhra Pradesh, is positioned as India's first coastal economic corridor and aims to integrate the Indian economy with global production networks in East and Southeast Asia, building on Andhra Pradesh's Industrial Development Policy (2015) and a comprehensive industrial sector roadmap prepared with ADB support. This program is critical for addressing challenges like the manufacturing sector's below-national-average contribution to GDP, low industrial diversification, and inadequate infrastructure and skills in Andhra Pradesh, despite the state ranking second nationally for ease of doing business.

ACTIVITY ID: XM-DAC-46004-51418-001-TA9682

ACTIVITY DESCRIPTION: The "Air Quality Improvement in the Greater Beijing–Tianjin–Hebei Region—Shandong Clean Heating and Cooling Project" is a sovereign project located in the People's Republic of China (PRC), with the Shandong Provincial Government as the Executing Agency. Assigned Project Number 51418-001 under ADB's EARD/EASI department, it aims to address the severe air quality issues in the greater Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei (BTH) region, particularly in Shandong Province, which are primarily attributed to an overreliance on coal for heavy industry, electricity, and heating. Shandong is the PRC's largest coal consumer, deriving 79.3% of its primary energy from coal, leading to high PM2.5 concentrations, as seen in Jinan city where coal use accounted for about 70% of total PM2.5 in 2016, reaching 84% during winter. The project aligns with national and provincial priorities to restructure the energy mix and reduce coal consumption, as outlined in the PRC's Thirteenth Five-Year Plan and Shandong's Action Plan for Air Pollution Control (2013–2020), which targets a reduction in annual average PM2.5 concentration levels to 49 µg/m³ by 2020. The project's impact is expected to be reduced air pollution and improved public health in the greater BTH region.

This initiative is categorized under the Energy sector, with subsectors including Conventional energy generation, Energy efficiency and conservation, Renewable energy generation (biomass and waste), and Renewable energy generation (geothermal). The strategic agenda supports inclusive economic growth by enhancing access to economic opportunities and job creation, while also promoting environmentally sustainable growth through eco-efficiency, addressing global/regional transboundary environmental concerns, and urban environmental improvement. It is classified for effective gender mainstreaming (EGM), with specific targets including at least 40% women staff reporting improved knowledge of operations and maintenance, and creating at least 700 new skilled and unskilled jobs for women. The project aims to achieve a significant CO2 reduction of 3,990,000 tons per annum, with a high climate change impact and $360 million in ADB mitigation financing. Governance and capacity development, knowledge solutions, and partnerships are key drivers, including institutional and organizational development, application of new knowledge solutions, and commercial cofinancing from the private sector. The project is "Complex" in risk categorization, with Environment: A, Involuntary Resettlement: C, and Indigenous Peoples: C safeguard categories. Geographic and household targeting are not applicable, but SDG targeting includes SDG3, SDG5, SDG7, and SDG11, with medium location impact for both rural and urban areas.

The project directly responds to the urgent need to reduce coal consumption and improve air quality by introducing more efficient methods and advanced technology for heat production, replacing coal with cleaner sources like natural gas and renewable energy, and reducing raw coal burning in households. Expected outcomes include sharp reductions in PM2.5, SO2, and NOx, especially benefiting rural households by saving lives and improving physical health. It is well-aligned with ADB's country partnership strategy for the PRC (2016–2020) and Strategy 2030, supporting climate change mitigation through advanced technologies, gender equality acceleration, and making cities more livable. Using a project loan modality, this initiative is narrowly focused on the heating and cooling industry in a concentrated geographic area (Jinan and Shanghe County), serving as a pilot with high potential for scale-up and duplication across the PRC and other developing member countries, supported by technical knowledge exchange sessions. Innovative features include advanced heating and cooling technologies with an innovative business model leveraging government incentives and heating company expertise to deliver cost-effective solutions to rural households, combining renewable energy, waste heat recovery, and supply/demand-side energy efficiency measures to reduce energy and carbon intensity, air pollution, and greenhouse gas emissions.

The total project cost is $710.76 million, with ADB financing providing $399.91 million as a Sovereign Project (Regular Loan) from ordinary capital resources, denominated in EUR (€350,000,000). Cofinancing is not explicitly stated as a separate component, but significant counterpart funding of $310.85 million comes from beneficiaries ($26.30 million), others ($139.47 million), and the project sponsor ($145.08 million). An additional technical assistance of $750,000 will be grant-financed by the Clean Energy Fund under the Clean Energy Financing Partnership Facility. The loan term is 25 years, including a 5-year grace period. Key activities include constructing biomass-based combined heat and power plants, retrofitting and expanding district heating networks, constructing geothermal heating systems, installing heat pumps in areas not connected to central heating, applying energy efficiency measures on urban buildings and rural households, constructing deep-well geothermal district heating systems, delivering improved heating sources, installing gas boilers, constructing large-scale district cooling systems, and developing thermal storage capacity. Beyond infrastructure, Output 4 focuses on strengthening clean heating awareness and capacity through campaigns, enhancing implementing agencies' knowledge of advanced technologies, and conducting participant feedback surveys. Risks identified include high financial leverage of Jinan Heating Group (JHG) and Jinan Thermal Power Co., Ltd. (JTPC), insufficient capacity for new technologies, adverse foreign exchange rate/interest rate movements, and unfamiliarity with ADB procurement guidelines. Mitigation measures include controlling financial leverage via loan covenants, providing technical assistance for capacity building, establishing a mandatory 1% loan amount reserve for currency/interest risks, and offering capacity training on ADB procurement requirements. The Governments of the PRC and Shandong Provincial Government have assured ADB of conformity with applicable policies, including anticorruption, safeguards, gender, procurement, consulting services, and disbursement.

ACTIVITY ID: XM-DAC-46004-53165-001-LN3881

ACTIVITY DESCRIPTION: The Energy Sector Reforms and Financial Sustainability Program (Subprogram 1), with project number 53165-001, is a complex activity in Pakistan, designated as a Sovereign Programmatic Approach Policy-Based Lending (Concessional Loan) using ordinary capital resources. The activity aims to reduce financial, technical, and governance deficits in Pakistan's energy sector, which adversely impact sector sustainability, affordability, fiscal balance, and macroeconomic stability. The Ministry of Finance is the executing agency. The total financing for this program is $380.00 million, with $300.00 million from ADB and $80.00 million cofinanced by the Export-Import Bank of Korea, all in US Dollars. The program incorporates lessons from previous ADB policy-based programs and aligns with the IMF's extended fund facility and ADB's Economic Stabilization Program, developed in consultation with key development partners. It is linked to ADB's Strategy 2030 operational priorities of gender equality, tackling climate change, and strengthening governance and institutional capacity, with some gender elements (SGE) and a general intervention on poverty. Climate change impact on the project is low, with $45.00 million of ADB financing allocated to mitigation.

To achieve its objectives, the program will focus on three main areas: securing financial sustainability by controlling and reducing circular debt; strengthening governance through rationalizing a competitive market roadmap, separating policy and regulatory functions in hydrocarbons, appointing appellate tribunals, implementing multiyear tariffs, and unbundling the gas subsector; and reinforcing infrastructure improvements through integrated planning to facilitate public and private sector investment across the energy supply chain. The sector is identified as Energy, with a subsector focus on energy sector development and institutional reform. Key Sustainable Development Goals supported include SDG 9.1, SDG 12.2, and SDG 13.a. The safeguard categorization for Environment, Involuntary Resettlement, and Indigenous Peoples is C.

The program addresses critical issues in Pakistan's energy sector, which contributed to a decline in GDP growth to 3.3% in FY2019, alongside unsustainable fiscal and current account deficits. Accumulated losses of large state-owned enterprises, particularly energy SOEs, have constrained the government's fiscal position. Historical issues include intermittent energy supply-demand imbalances due to a lack of integrated planning, an uneven generation mix, and inadequate coordination of policies for renewable energy, leading to reliance on high-cost imported fuels. Previous reform efforts, such as the Accelerating Economic Transformation Program (AETP) and the SESRP, yielded some achievements like automatic fuel cost adjustment and tariff rationalization, but faced significant setbacks due to the IMF program going off-track, court cases stalling tariff reforms, and challenges in privatization, leading to a current liquidity crisis.

The new government has developed reform action plans in its new electricity policy and concept of integrated energy planning, which this program will support. Specific energy sector reforms include preparing a Circular Debt Reduction Plan with ADB, IMF, and World Bank consultation; adopting annual tariffs for FY2018 and FY2019 to recover Rs 469 billion in delayed costs; establishing an appellate tribunal under the National Electric Power Regulatory Authority (NEPRA) Act; formulating the National Electricity Policy to enable cost-effective generation diversification and enhance employment opportunities for women; formulating the Renewable Energy Policy 2019 for low-cost clean energy; approving a roadmap for a market-oriented electricity trading model; approving the privatization of two liquefied natural gas-fired power plants; enhancing the energy sector supply chain by separating hydrocarbon policy and regulatory functions and establishing the Pakistan Petroleum Exploration and Production Regulatory Authority; and ratifying Unidentified Gas Reduction Plans and approving an incentive policy for increasing oil and gas block bid rounds. These comprehensive reforms indicate a structured approach to tackle the deep-seated issues within the sector.
